title: jstats
text:
The Quick Start raced to a first result, and Loading, Saving, Converting Data covered getting files in and out. This page puts those pieces together and shows what an ordinary working session actually looks like вҖ” start to finish. The point isnвҖҷt any one command; itвҖҷs the order: how a real analysis moves from opening the data to a saved result, and where the everyday вҖңworking comfortablyвҖқ tools fall into place along the way.
It runs on the shipped practice datasets, so you can follow along with nothing of your own: community for the main thread, with a short detour to its messy companion clinic for the one step that needs a dirty column. Swap in your own data wherever you like вҖ” the moves are the same.






TipNew to R? Read this first





You donвҖҷt need to understand every line below to get the shape of a session. Read it once straight through for the story вҖ” load, look, tidy, analyze, save вҖ” and donвҖҷt worry if a particular function is new. Each one has its own help page (type ? and the name, e.g.В ?jdesc), and the earlier Get Started pages introduce the basics. The goal here is the arc, not mastery of every step.

title: jstats
section: Open the data and get your bearings
text:
Open the data and get your bearings
Every session starts by getting the data in front of you and taking a first look. The community data ships with the package, so itвҖҷs there by name the moment you run library(jstats) вҖ” no importing needed. (Opening a file of your own is covered in Loading, Saving, Converting Data.) juse() sets it as the session default, so later commands donвҖҷt need to be told which data frame to use every time.

juse(community)

Default data frame set to: community








TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





juse() plays the role StataвҖҷs use does: it names the active dataset once, and the analysis commands that follow assume it. In SPSS terms, itвҖҷs a bit like having a single data file open in Data View that every menu command then acts on вҖ” except you can load several data frames at once and switch which is the default whenever you like.



With the data loaded, jscreen() is the orientation pass вҖ” one call that lists every variable, its type, its range, and anything that looks off (odd values, missing data). stats = TRUE adds a center-and-spread column for the numeric variables.

jscreen(stats = TRUE)
Data Screening
Using default data frame: community
  Cases: 103 
  Variables: 15 
  Cases with missing data: 34 
  Variables with outliers: 0 

Variable Types
Variable        jstats Class  Sub-class   Unique Values       Mean  Median
--------------  ------------  ----------  -------------  ---------  ------
RespondentID    Categorical   identifier            103                   
Income          Numeric                              49  49855.670   49000
Education       Categorical   5-category              5

WellbeingScore  Numeric                              41     50.893      50

OwnsHome        Categorical   dichotomy*              2      1.534        
Smoker          Categorical   dichotomy               2      0.337

Variable      Missing  % Missing
------------  -------  ---------
Income              6        5.8
Education           6        5.8

A screen like this is where you catch problems early вҖ” a variable that should be a 0/1 dichotomy showing values of 1 and 2, say, or a вҖңscoreвҖқ whose minimum is an impossible negative number. That second one is exactly what we tackle next.

title: jstats
text:
A column that arrived messy
community ships clean, which is convenient but not realistic вҖ” real data usually need some tidying before they can be trusted. So for this one step we detour to clinic, jstatsвҖҷs deliberately messy companion dataset (also shipped, so itвҖҷs available by name too), clean a column there, and then return to community for the rest of the session.

juse(clinic)

Default data frame set to: clinic


The column MoodRating is meant to run from 1 to 10, but it arrived with two stand-in codes buried in it as ordinary numbers: -99 for вҖңRefusedвҖқ and -98 for вҖңDonвҖҷt knowвҖқ вҖ” the usual state of affairs after a plain CSV or Excel import, where the file carries no record of what those numbers mean. Until we tell jstats they stand for missing, they are treated as real values and quietly poison every statistic:

jdesc(MoodRating)
Descriptive Statistics
Using default data frame: clinic

Variable    Total  Non_missing  Min  Max    Mean      SD
----------  -----  -----------  ---  ---  ------  ------
MoodRating     70           70  -99    9  -4.943  31.477


The mean is dragged far below the 1вҖ“10 range, because those large negative codes are being averaged in as if they were genuine ratings. jdeclare_udm() fixes this the careful way вҖ” it declares the two codes as missing, with labels, without overwriting or deleting anything. The underlying data are untouched; weвҖҷve only told jstats how to read them.

clinic <- jdeclare_udm(clinic, MoodRating,
                       codes = c("Refused" = -99, "Don't know" = -98))

Declared SPSS-style missing values in:
  clinic$MoodRating
  -99 ["Refused"]
  -98 ["Don't know"]

Assign the result to keep the declaration:
  clinic <- jdeclare_udm(clinic, MoodRating, ...)

To keep it across sessions, save the data frame:
  jsave(clinic, "clinic.rds")

jdesc(MoodRating)
Descriptive Statistics
Using default data frame: clinic

Case Processing  Excluded  Remaining
    Original            вҖ”         70
    Remaining N         вҖ”         70

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ


Variable    Total  Non_missing  Min  Max  Mean     SD
----------  -----  -----------  ---  ---  ----  -----
MoodRating     70           63    1    9  5.46  1.702


Now the codes are set aside as missing and the mean sits sensibly inside the 1вҖ“10 range.






TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





This is the equivalent of setting a variableвҖҷs missing values in SPSSвҖҷs Variable View (the MISSING VALUES command in syntax), or StataвҖҷs extended missing values. The important parallel is that itвҖҷs non-destructive: like declaring missing values rather than recoding them to system-missing, jdeclare_udm() leaves the original codes in place and simply flags them, so nothing is lost and the declaration can be changed or undone later.









NoteGoing deeper (optional)





Declaring missing values is a small move with a lot behind it вҖ” different programs (SPSS, Stata, SAS) record missing data in different ways, and jstats keeps those distinctions straight when you read and write files. Loading, Saving, Converting Data covers the import/export side, and Book 2 covers the cross-program fidelity in more detail, with worked examples. The help page ?jdeclare_udm documents the options.




juse(community)

Default data frame set to: community


With the detour done, community is the session default again.

title: jstats
text:
Build a scale
A common early task is combining several survey items into one scale score вҖ” but only after checking the items hang together. community has a five-item Environment battery (Environment1 through Environment5).
By default jstats prints variable names, which keeps output uncluttered. But a variableвҖҷs label, when it has one, usually carries the actual question wording вҖ” and that can be a useful clue when something looks off. So before we start, letвҖҷs switch the display to bring the labels along. joutput() sets output preferences for the rest of the session; variable.id = "legend" keeps the short names in the tables and adds a label legend beneath them:

joutput(variable.id = "legend")
Output Settings
Level: standard
  effect.size: ON
  regression.ci: OFF
  means.ci: ON
  levene: OFF
  posthoc: OFF
  diagnostics: OFF
  case.processing: AUTO
  case.processing.detail: TOTALS
  variable.id: LEGEND (override)
  value.id: BOTH
  ref.categories: ON
  udm.notice: ON
  digits: 3


Running joutput() prints its full settings panel вҖ” every output setting, not only the one you changed вҖ” so the screenful you see here is expected, not a sign anything went wrong. The setting you changed, variable.id, is now marked (override), flagging that it differs from the default; the others stay at their usual values.
You can also set this for a single call, by passing variable.id = "legend" to that function directly instead of session-wide.






NoteGoing deeper (optional)





variable.id is just one of several display settings joutput() controls. ?joutput lists them all, and the Reference page links every function to its own help. We wonвҖҷt work through every setting here вҖ” the guides are a starting point, not a complete manual вҖ” so for the output options covered in more detail, with worked examples, see Book 2.



Now jalpha() reports CronbachвҖҷs alpha and the per-item diagnostics, with each itemвҖҷs label shown in the legend below:

jalpha(Environment1, Environment2, Environment3, Environment4, Environment5)
Reliability Analysis
Using default data frame: community

Case Processing    Excluded  Remaining
    Original              вҖ”        103
    Auto-listwise        19         84
    Analysis N            вҖ”         84

Missing-data breakdown  From 103     %
    Environment1
      Missing              12     11.7
    Environment3
      Missing              12     11.7

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

Reliability Statistics
Cronbach's Alpha  N of Items
----------------  ----------
           0.348           5

Item Statistics
Item           Mean     SD   N
------------  -----  -----  --
Environment1  3.131  1.180  84
Environment2  2.869  1.180  84
Environment3  3.060  1.226  84
Environment4  3.333  1.186  84
Environment5  2.762  1.470  84


Warning: The following item(s) are negatively correlated with the rest of the scale: Environment2.
They may need reverse-coding, or may not belong in the scale - check the item-total table and the item wording.



Item-Total Statistics
Item          Corrected Item-Total r  Alpha if Item Deleted
------------  ----------------------  ---------------------
Environment1                   0.510                 -0.006
Environment2                  -0.660                  0.783
Environment3                   0.539                 -0.052
Environment4                   0.447                  0.056
Environment5                   0.455                 -0.030

Variable Labels:
  Environment1 = Climate change is a serious threat.
  Environment2 = Concern about the environment is exaggerated. R
  Environment3 = Government should do more for the environment.
  Environment4 = I would pay more for environmentally friendly products.
  Environment5 = Pollution is a major cause of public health problems.


jalpha() flags Environment2 as reverse-keyed: it correlates negatively with the rest, which drags the reliability down. The label legend tells you why вҖ” Environment2вҖҷs label ends in вҖңRвҖқ, the marker this datasetвҖҷs author used for a reverse-coded item. Your own data wonвҖҷt always be flagged so conveniently, but the label wording is often the tell on its own: a positively phrased item sitting among negatively phrased ones is exactly the kind of thing that surfaces here. We fix it by recoding the item into a new column that runs the same direction as the others. jrecode() returns the recoded variable, which we store as a new column:

community$Environment2R <- jrecode(community, Environment2,
                                   map = "1=5; 2=4; 3=3; 4=2; 5=1")


Note: jrecode() returns the recoded values; assign them to a column to keep them:
  community$<name> <- jrecode(...)
To check the recode landed correctly, compare jfreq() on the original and the new column.








TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





That map = "1=5; 2=4; ..." is the same idea as SPSSвҖҷs RECODE Environment2 (1=5)(2=4)(3=3)(4=2)(5=1) INTO Environment2R. Storing the result in a new column (Environment2R) rather than overwriting the original is the safe habit вҖ” the original item stays intact in case you need it.



Run jalpha() again with the reversed item in place of the original:

jalpha(Environment1, Environment2R, Environment3, Environment4, Environment5)
Reliability Analysis
Using default data frame: community

Case Processing    Excluded  Remaining
    Original              вҖ”        103
    Auto-listwise        19         84
    Analysis N            вҖ”         84

Missing-data breakdown  From 103     %
    Environment1
      Missing              12     11.7
    Environment3
      Missing              12     11.7

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ

Reliability Statistics
Cronbach's Alpha  N of Items
----------------  ----------
           0.827           5

Item Statistics
Item            Mean     SD   N
-------------  -----  -----  --
Environment1   3.131  1.180  84
Environment2R  3.131  1.180  84
Environment3   3.060  1.226  84
Environment4   3.333  1.186  84
Environment5   2.762  1.470  84

Item-Total Statistics
Item           Corrected Item-Total r  Alpha if Item Deleted
-------------  ----------------------  ---------------------
Environment1                    0.691                  0.774
Environment2R                   0.660                  0.783
Environment3                    0.694                  0.773
Environment4                    0.679                  0.778
Environment5                    0.445                  0.854

Variable Labels:
  Environment1  = Climate change is a serious threat.
  Environment2R = Concern about the environment is exaggerated. R (recoded)
  Environment3  = Government should do more for the environment.
  Environment4  = I would pay more for environmentally friendly products.
  Environment5  = Pollution is a major cause of public health problems.


With the item reversed, reliability improves вҖ” but the diagnostics now point at Environment5: it has the weakest corrected item-total correlation and the highest вҖңalpha if item dropped,вҖқ meaning the scale would be a touch more reliable without it. So we drop it and build the scale from the four items that hang together, as a per-case mean with javg(), requiring at least three of the four present so a respondent who skipped one still gets a score:

community$EnvScale <- javg(Environment1, Environment2R, Environment3, Environment4,
                           min.valid = 3, var.label = "Environment scale (mean)")

Mean of 4 variables computed for 103 cases (min.valid = 3: 14 cases used partial data, 5 set to NA due to missing values).
Mean of the new variable: 3.178.



Note: javg() returns the scores; assign them to a column to keep them:
  community$<name> <- javg(...)
For the full distribution (min, max, SD), run jdesc() on the new column.

jdesc(EnvScale)
Descriptive Statistics
Using default data frame: community

Case Processing  Excluded  Remaining
    Original            вҖ”        103
    Remaining N         вҖ”        103

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ


Variable  Total  Non_missing  Min  Max   Mean     SD
--------  -----  -----------  ---  ---  -----  -----
EnvScale    103           98    1    5  3.178  0.973

Variable Labels:
  EnvScale = Environment scale (mean)








NoteGoing deeper (optional)





Book 1 covers what CronbachвҖҷs alpha measures and why a reverse-keyed item has to be flipped before itвҖҷs trusted; Book 2 covers jalpha()вҖҷs and javg()вҖҷs options вҖ” the min.valid cutoff, alternative reliability handling вҖ” in more detail, with worked examples. The help pages ?jalpha, ?jrecode, and ?javg document them as well.

title: jstats
text:
Run the analysis
With a clean, prepared dataset, the analysis itself is short. jstats uses the same DV ~ IV formula youвҖҷd write in base R, so a linear regression of wellbeing on income and age reads naturally:

jlm(WellbeingScore ~ Income + Age)
Linear Regression
Using default data frame: community

Case Processing    Excluded  Remaining
    Original              вҖ”        103
    Auto-listwise         6         97
    Analysis N            вҖ”         97

Missing-data breakdown  From 103    %
    Income
      Missing              6      5.8

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ


Coefficients
               b      SE      t      ОІ      p  
-----------  ------  -----  -----  -----  -----
(Intercept)  24.815  3.749  6.620         <.001
Income        0.000  0.000  6.813  0.564  <.001
Age           0.181  0.082  2.208  0.183   .030

Outcome:
  WellbeingScore = Wellbeing score (0-100)
Predictors:
  Income         = Annual income (USD)
  Age            = Age (years)

R-squared: 0.411    Adjusted R-squared: 0.398
Residual Standard Error: 9.013

F-statistic: 32.729 on 2 and 94 DF, p-value: <.001
Sum of Squares:
  Regression: 5317.416
  Residual:   7635.945
  Total:      12953.361


To add a categorical predictor like Region, you tell jstats to treat it as a set of dummy variables. jdummy() registers that once вҖ” choosing West as the reference category here вҖ” and every analysis that follows honors it, so you donвҖҷt re-specify it each time:

jdummy(Region, ref = "West")
Dummy Variable Registration
Using default data frame: community

  Variable: Region (haven_labelled)
  Reference category: Region_West
  Dummy variables: Region_North, Region_South, Region_East
  Cases: 103 (0 missing)


Note: this registration is stored for this session only.
To keep it across sessions, save the data frame in R format (.rds):
  jsave(community, "community.rds")

Next session, load that file to restore the registration:
  community <- jload("community.rds")

jlm(WellbeingScore ~ Income + Age + Region)
Linear Regression
Using default data frame: community

Case Processing    Excluded  Remaining
    Original              вҖ”        103
    Auto-listwise         6         97
    Analysis N            вҖ”         97

Missing-data breakdown  From 103    %
    Income

TipFor experienced R users





jdummy() is doing what youвҖҷd otherwise arrange with factor() and contrasts() / relevel(). The difference is that the registration is non-destructive and persistent: Region stays a plain labelled variable in the data, and the dummy treatment lives in a separate registration that jlm(), jlogistic(), and the others look up вҖ” so you set the reference category in one place, not inside every model call.



Logistic regression works the same way, with a 0/1 outcome. Volunteer is already coded 0/1, so it drops straight in:

jlogistic(Volunteer ~ Age)
Logistic Regression
Using default data frame: community

Coefficients
               b      SE    Wald   df   p    Exp(B)
-----------  ------  -----  -----  --  ----  ------
(Intercept)  -2.347  0.811  8.382   1  .004   0.096
Age           0.055  0.019  8.216   1  .004   1.057

Outcome:
  Volunteer = Volunteered in past year
Predictors:
  Age       = Age (years)

Omnibus Test of Model Coefficients
Chi-Square  df  p   
----------  --  ----
     9.283   1  .002

Model Summary
-2 Log Likelihood  Cox & Snell RВІ  Nagelkerke RВІ      AIC
-----------------  --------------  -------------  -------
          133.263           0.086          0.115  137.263

Dependent Variable Encoding
  Modeled (1):   Yes
  Reference (0): No


A dichotomy stored as 1/2 (the common Yes/No convention) needs recoding to 0/1 first вҖ” jstats will stop with a message telling you exactly that, rather than running something misleading. The recode is a one-liner with jrecode(), the same tool we used on the scale item; Loading, Saving, Converting Data shows that step in context.






NoteGoing deeper (optional)





Book 1 covers the regression ideas themselves вҖ” what the coefficients mean, how dummy variables encode a categorical predictor; Book 2 covers jlm() and jlogistic()вҖҷs options and the reference-category choice in more detail, with worked examples. ?jlm, ?jlogistic, and ?jdummy document the options too.

title: jstats
section: Keep one set of cases across analyses
text:
Keep one set of cases across analyses
When several variables have missing values, different analyses can quietly run on different subsets of respondents вҖ” a regression on these three variables uses a different N than a correlation on those four. When you want a run of analyses to share the same respondents, jcomplete() sets a listwise filter: only cases complete on the variables you name are used, until you turn it off.

jcomplete(Income, Education, Age)
Listwise Case Filter
Using default data frame: community

Variable     N  Missing  % Missing
---------  ---  -------  ---------
Income     103        6  5.8%     
Education  103        6  5.8%     
Age        103        0  0.0%     

  Complete cases: 91 of 103 (88.3%)
  Listwise filter activated вҖ” 12 cases will be excluded from subsequent analyses.

jdesc(Age)
Descriptive Statistics
Using default data frame: community

Case Processing  Excluded  Remaining
    Original            вҖ”        103
    jcomplete          12         91  Income, Education, +1 more
    Remaining N         вҖ”         91

в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ


Variable  Total  Non_missing  Min  Max    Mean      SD
--------  -----  -----------  ---  ---  ------  ------
Age          91           91   18   71  40.484  11.933

Variable Labels:
  Age = Age (years)


Every analysis you run now uses just that matching set of cases, so their NвҖҷs line up and the results are comparable. (jcomplete() can also preview exactly which cases it would drop before you commit вҖ” ?jcomplete shows how.)

title: jstats
section: A clean slate when you want one
text:
A clean slate when you want one
The session default, the dummy registration, and the listwise filter all persist by design вҖ” thatвҖҷs what makes them convenient. When you do want to clear them (starting a genuinely separate analysis, or undoing something), each has an off switch:

jcomplete(NULL)            # drop the listwise filter

jcomplete cleared for community (had: Income, Education, Age).

jdummy(community, NULL)    # drop the dummy registrations

Dummy registrations cleared for community: Region.

juse(NULL)                 # clear the session default

Default data frame cleared.


You wonвҖҷt normally clear these between ordinary analyses вҖ” leaving a default and your registrations in place is the point of having them. ItвҖҷs for a fresh start, or to undo a mistake.

title: jstats
section: Dialing the detail up or down
text:
Dialing the detail up or down
Everything youвҖҷve seen this session came out at jstatsвҖҷs standard detail level вҖ” the default. joutput() also takes an overall level, so you can trim the output or expand it in a single move:

joutput("minimal")    # core results only вҖ” leanest output
joutput("standard")   # the default you've been seeing
joutput("full")       # fullest detail вҖ” diagnostics, extra tests, complete processing summaries

minimal strips the output down to the essential result tables вҖ” handy for a clean report or a production script; full turns on the additional diagnostics and notes that standard leaves off. Important warnings and errors always appear, whatever level you choose.






NoteGoing deeper (optional)





Each level is a preset for a whole set of individual switches вҖ” effect sizes, confidence intervals, the Case Processing Summary, and more вҖ” and you can override any one of them on its own, as you did earlier with variable.id. ?joutput lists every setting and what each level turns on; Book 2 covers output control in more detail, with worked examples.

title: jstats
section: Save your work, and open a file someone sends you
text:
Save your work, and open a file someone sends you
A finished session usually ends by saving the prepared data. jsave() writes the in-memory data frame back out to a file, and jload() reads it back; together theyвҖҷre the round trip. Across formats вҖ” RвҖҷs own .rds, plus SPSS, Stata, and Excel вҖ” jstats carries value labels, variable labels, and missing-value declarations along as faithfully as each format allows.

jsave(community, "community.rds")     # save the prepared data
jload("community", overwrite = TRUE)  # read it back







NoteGoing deeper (optional)





Saving across formats is where the missing-value distinctions earn their keep вҖ” an SPSS-style code canвҖҷt write straight to a Stata file, and Excel stores no missing-value metadata at all, so jstats handles (and warns about) each case. Loading, Saving, Converting Data walks the full cross-format round trip live; Book 2 covers the fidelity rules in more detail, with worked examples. ?jsave and ?jconvert document the options.



Everything above used files youвҖҷd save yourself. The package also ships an example SPSS file inside itself вҖ” a stand-in for a data file a colleague emails you. system.file() is base RвҖҷs way of finding a file bundled inside an installed package; jload() then opens it like any other received file, missing-value codes and all:

received <- system.file("extdata", "community_spss.sav", package = "jstats")
jload(received, name = "community_received", overwrite = TRUE)

Loaded community_received (SPSS format; 103 cases, 15 variables)


5 variables have user-defined missing values:
  Income: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Education: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Smoker: -99 ["Refused"]
  Environment1: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Environment3: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
These codes are excluded as missing in jstats analyses. For better base R compatibility, convert them:
  jconvert(community_received, to = "stata")  - retains missing-value codes, base R compatible (recommended)
  jconvert(community_received, to = "baseR")  - converts to plain NA and removes missing-value codes

jscreen(community_received, stats = TRUE)

And thatвҖҷs a full session, end to end: opened the data, screened it, cleaned a messy column, built and checked a scale, run the models, kept the case base consistent, and saved the result вҖ” with the everyday conveniences (juse(), jdummy(), jcomplete()) falling into place as they came up rather than as separate lessons.
This page is the narrative of a session, not a manual: to keep it moving it left a good deal unexplained вҖ” including output you saw but we didnвҖҷt stop on, like the Case Processing Summary that headed several of the analyses (the small table reporting how many cases an analysis used and how missing values were handled). For looking a single task up while you work вҖ” вҖңhow do I reverse-code an item,вҖқ вҖңhow do I get a correlation matrixвҖқ вҖ” see the Reference page, which links every function to its full help and its options. And to go further than these guides, the book series covers the analyses and their output in full: Book 1 builds the statistics and R together from the start, and Book 2 is the option-by-option guide вҖ” output settings, the Case Processing Summary, and the rest вҖ” for readers who already know the statistics.

section: Security warnings when downloading or installing
text:
Security warnings when downloading or installing
Installing R or RStudio for the first time can bring up a warning or two вҖ” a browser prompt, a blue вҖңWindows protected your PCвҖқ box, or a request for a password on a work machine. This is normal for brand-new software from a source your computer hasnвҖҷt seen you use before; none of it means anything is wrong. Open the box that matches your situation:






WarningOn your own computer





When an installer finishes downloading, your browser may ask whether to keep the file. Keep it вҖ” itвҖҷs the installer you just requested.
Running the R installer, Windows may show a blue SmartScreen box offering, at first, only a DonвҖҷt run button. This is reputation-based, not a virus alert: it appears because the file is newly released and too few people have downloaded it yet for Windows to recognize it вҖ” not because anything is wrong with it. Click More info and the box expands to show the app name and the publisher, along with a Run anyway button. Only ever do this for installers you downloaded yourself from the official sources вҖ” cran.r-project.org for R and posit.co for RStudio.



The version numbers you see will be newer than the ones shown here.


On some machines вҖ” most often university- or employer-managed ones вҖ” the blue box appears with no More info link at all: just a DonвҖҷt run button and no way past it. This usually happens when the installer is launched from the browserвҖҷs download list. The fix is to launch it from your Downloads folder instead: open File Explorer, go to Downloads, and double-click the installer there. If the box still offers no way through even from the Downloads folder, youвҖҷre in the managed-computer situation вҖ” see the next box down.

Next, Windows asks permission to make changes to your computer вҖ” the User Account Control box. It opens with No highlighted, so you have to click Yes on purpose. Where it names a verified publisher, thatвҖҷs Windows confirming the installerвҖҷs digital signature checked out. (RStudioвҖҷs permission box looks the same. You generally wonвҖҷt see the SmartScreen step for RStudio, because its publisher is already widely recognized.)



The version numbers you see will be newer than the ones shown here.


One last thing, if you ever download an installer twice: the second copy comes back with a number added to its name, like R-4.6.1-win (1).exe. Any copy is fine to run вҖ” the (1) is just your browser avoiding a name clash, not a different or altered program.









WarningOn a work or managed computer





On a university or workplace computer you may not be allowed to install software yourself. What happens depends on how the machine is set up:

The install is blocked, but the screen offers a path to temporary administrator rights вҖ” usually a prompt for your own credentials, sometimes followed by a box asking why youвҖҷre installing. Both are routine; fill them in and carry on.
ThereвҖҷs no self-service path, in which case R and RStudio are requested through your IT service desk, a software catalog, or your instructor. The note below is written for whoever handles that request вҖ” you can point them straight to it.










WarningA note for your IT department





The rest of this entry is addressed to IT staff.
A student or researcher has asked to install R and RStudio Desktop, the standard open-source tools for statistical computing and the environment these guides teach. This note summarizes their provenance to support a software-approval assessment.

Sources. R is published by the R Foundation through CRAN (cran.r-project.org); RStudio Desktop comes from Posit (posit.co / docs.posit.co). Both are long-established and in wide academic and government use.
Signatures and integrity. The R for Windows installer is code-signed вҖ” the User Account Control dialog shows a verified publisher вҖ” and it is downloaded over HTTPS from CRAN, which also publishes checksums for verifying the download. RStudio Desktop is signed by Posit Software, PBC.
Installing without administrator rights. Administrator rights arenвҖҷt strictly required. Run without them, the R for Windows installer installs into the userвҖҷs own file area instead of Program Files (administrator rights are needed only for a system-wide install and the optional registry entries). RStudio Desktop is additionally offered as a Windows .zip archive that can be unpacked and run without the installer; current deployment guidance is on PositвҖҷs documentation site.
SmartScreen. Microsoft SmartScreen may flag a freshly released version until it accumulates download reputation; this resets with each release and is not an indication of malware.
Organizations operating under standards such as ISO/IEC 27001 will route this through their software-approval process; the information above is provided to support that assessment.

title: jstats
text:
Installing jstats






WarningA red WARNING says Rtools is required





During the install, the Console may print a red WARNING that Rtools is required to build R packages. It looks alarming, but itвҖҷs normal and not an error: itвҖҷs RStudioвҖҷs general-purpose check for build tools, and it fires on almost any package install on a Windows computer that doesnвҖҷt have Rtools. jstats doesnвҖҷt need Rtools вҖ” it contains no compiled code and installs as a ready-made binary вҖ” so the install completes and jstats runs fine. You can ignore the warning; thereвҖҷs nothing to fix.









WarningThe install seems half-done or broken





The simplest fix is to remove jstats and install it fresh:
remove.packages("jstats")
install.packages("jstats",
  repos = c("https://jma61.r-universe.dev", "https://cloud.r-project.org"))
library(jstats)









WarningThe install fails on Windows: вҖҳnot writableвҖҷ, вҖҳcannot create dirвҖҷ, or the message mentions OneDrive





On many Windows computers the Documents folder is synced by OneDrive вҖ” and by default that is where R keeps its package library (the folder your installed packages live in). OneDrive can lock files while it syncs, and an install that runs into a lock fails part-way with messages like the ones above.
Fixes, from simplest to most durable:

Pause OneDrive and retry. Click the OneDrive cloud icon near the clock, choose Pause syncing, run the install line again, then resume syncing.
Give R a package library outside the synced folder. This one-time setup ends the problem for good. Create a plain folder directly on your drive, such as C:\Rlibs. Then create a plain text file named .Renviron in your Documents folder containing this single line:
R_LIBS_USER=C:/Rlibs
(Note the forward slash вҖ” thatвҖҷs how R writes paths.) Restart R and run the install line again; packages now install to the new folder, out of OneDriveвҖҷs reach.

A related, rarer cause of the same errors: a Windows username containing accented or non-English characters can break the library path in the same way. The same fix applies вҖ” a package library at a simple location like C:/Rlibs.









WarningThe install asks for an administrator password, or a work network blocks the download





On managed work or lab computers, two things can stop the install: your account may not be allowed to install software, or the network may block downloads from sites it doesnвҖҷt recognize. Neither is anything you did wrong вҖ” and the request to your IT support is a small one. jstats installs as a ready-made binary (no compilers or build tools are needed), fetched from the two addresses in the install line: the r-universe repository (jma61.r-universe.dev) and an ordinary CRAN mirror (cloud.r-project.org). Ask IT either to allow those two addresses or to run the install line for you; either way itвҖҷs a quick job for them.









WarningWhere did jstats install? / I updated, but I still see the old version





R keeps installed packages in a library вҖ” a folder on your computer вҖ” and there can be more than one. On a fresh Windows setup, the install often lands quietly in RвҖҷs system library (inside Program Files) rather than in your personal library, without asking. On its own thatвҖҷs harmless: jstats installs and runs fine from there.
It only matters if you end up with two copies in two libraries вҖ” then an update can land in one library while R keeps loading the older copy from the other, and you appear stuck on the old version. To see which copy R is using, and every library R searches:
find.package("jstats")   # the folder of the copy R loads
.libPaths()              # every library R searches, in order
If those reveal a second, older copy, remove it from the library it sits in and restart R:
remove.packages("jstats", lib = "paste-the-other-library-path-here")
Then load jstats again and confirm the version with packageVersion("jstats").

title: jstats
text:
Starting RStudio and loading jstats






WarningRStudio hangs or loops every time it starts





This almost always means an install command was put in your .Rprofile. That file runs every time R starts, so an install line tries to reinstall on every startup and can loop. Your .Rprofile should only ever hold lightweight startup lines like library(jstats) вҖ” never an install command. To recover: close RStudio, open your Project folder in your computerвҖҷs file manager, open the .Rprofile file in a plain text editor (Notepad on Windows, TextEdit on Mac), delete the install line, keeping the library(jstats) line (and its reminder comment, if your .Rprofile has one), save, and reopen RStudio.









WarningLoading jstats pauses for several seconds on a no-internet connection





While jstats is still in development it installs from an r-universe repository, and each time it loads it briefly checks that repository for a newer version. The package itself works fully offline, but on a machine that is connected to a network yet has no route to the open internet вҖ“ common on locked-down lab or server installations вҖ“ that check has to time out before loading finishes, so library(jstats) can pause for a few seconds.
To skip the check and load immediately, set this option before loading the package:
options(jstats.check_updates = FALSE)
library(jstats)
Putting the options(...) line above the library(jstats) line in your .Rprofile makes it the default every session. On an ordinary internet connection you can leave it out вҖ“ the check is fast there, and itвҖҷs how you learn an update is available.
This is specific to this pre-CRAN version. Once jstats reaches CRAN, installing and updating happen the normal CRAN way, with no startup check of this kind.









WarningError in library(jstats): there is no package called вҖҳjstatsвҖҷ





This shows up two ways: you run library(jstats) in the Console and get this error, or вҖ” if your .Rprofile loads jstats automatically вҖ” the same error greets you every time RStudio starts. Either way, the usual cause is that R itself was updated, and updating R starts a fresh, empty package library вҖ” so jstats (along with any other packages you had installed) is no longer there.
The rule in plain words: RвҖҷs version numbers have three parts, like 4.5.2. Only a change to the middle number (4.5 вҶ’ 4.6) starts a fresh package library; a last-number update (4.5.1 вҶ’ 4.5.2) changes nothing about your packages. And this is about R, not RStudio вҖ” RStudio updates are always safe and never touch your packages. R has no built-in update notifier, so updating R is a separate, deliberate act; if you did it recently, this reinstall is the one follow-up chore.
(Why jupdate() canвҖҷt rescue you here: jupdate() is itself a jstats command, and jstats isnвҖҷt installed in the new library yet.)
The fix is the same install line as the first time вҖ” run it once and youвҖҷre back:
install.packages("jstats",
  repos = c("https://jma61.r-universe.dev", "https://cloud.r-project.org"))
library(jstats)
DonвҖҷt confuse this error with the harmless warning that jstats was built under R version 4.x.x вҖ” that one just means the ready-made copy was built on a slightly different R patch, needs no action, and clears on its own. This callout is about the actual error where jstats canвҖҷt be found at all.









Warningcould not find function вҖҳjfreqвҖҷ (or another jstats function)





jstats probably isnвҖҷt loaded in this session. Run:
library(jstats)
If it still isnвҖҷt found, check spelling and capitalization вҖ” R is case-sensitive, so jfreq and jFreq are different names.









Warningobject вҖҳcommunityвҖҷ not found





The shipped datasets become available once the package is loaded. Run library(jstats) first, then try again. (If youвҖҷre working with your own data instead, make sure youвҖҷve loaded it вҖ” see Loading, Saving, Converting Data.)

title: jstats
text:
Typing commands in the Console






WarningThe Console shows a + and nothing runs





If you press Enter and the Console answers with a + instead of the usual > prompt, R thinks your command isnвҖҷt finished вҖ” nearly always a closing quotation mark or a closing parenthesis is missing. Press the Escape key (Esc) to cancel and get a fresh > prompt, then retype or re-paste the whole command. (Typing more into the + line rarely ends well; Escape and a clean start is quicker.)









WarningError: unexpected symbol (or unexpected вҖҳ,вҖҷ or вҖҳ)вҖҷ)





These errors are RвҖҷs way of saying the punctuation in a command doesnвҖҷt add up вҖ” most often a missing comma between two inputs, or a missing or extra parenthesis or quotation mark. Compare your line character by character against the example you copied it from; the difference is usually one small mark.
A reading tip that helps with R errors in general: in a long error message, the most useful part usually comes after the final colon вҖ” start reading there, then work backward only if you need to.

title: jstats
text: Before installing the package, itвҖҷs worth a few minutes getting comfortable with RStudio itself вҖ” where things are, and how your data looks once itвҖҷs loaded. If you already work in RStudio day to day, you can skim this and move on to Install jstats.

title: jstats
text:
Start a Project
Before installing the package or loading any data, create an RStudio Project.
Without a Project, R needs to be told where your files live (its вҖңworking directoryвҖқ), and that quickly drags in folder paths, the difference between \ and /, and the fact that Windows and Mac write paths differently. A Project sidesteps all of it: it ties a folder, your data, and your work together, so R always knows where it is.
To create one, in RStudio choose File вҶ’ New Project вҶ’ New Directory вҶ’ New Project, give it a name (for example jstats-practice), pick where it should live вҖ” a folder on your computer itself is a safer home than a cloud-synced one like OneDrive, whose syncing can quietly interfere with RвҖҷs files вҖ” and click Create Project. RStudio opens your new Project.
From now on, there are two ways to reopen this Project. From inside RStudio, choose File вҶ’ Open Project. Or, in your computerвҖҷs file manager вҖ” File Explorer on Windows, Finder on a Mac вҖ” open the Project folder and double-click its .Rproj file (the small file RStudio created there), which launches straight into RStudio. That one habit вҖ” always work inside a Project вҖ” saves more beginner headaches than any other.

title: jstats
text:
The RStudio panes
With your Project open, RStudio shows three panes to begin with. A fourth вҖ” the Source pane, in the top-left вҖ” doesnвҖҷt appear until the first time you open a script or view a dataset, so weвҖҷll meet it last. WeвҖҷll take the other three in the order youвҖҷll actually reach for them, starting on the left.

The Console вҖ” where you type and run code
The tall pane down the left side is the Console. (With only three panes open it fills the whole left side; the moment the Source pane appears above it, the Console settles into the bottom-left.) This is where you type вҖ” or paste вҖ” a command, press Enter, and R runs it, printing the result just below.
LetвҖҷs make that real. Click anywhere in the Console, type a sum, and press Enter:
2 + 2
R answers straight back:
[1] 4
The Console is a calculator that talks back. Now try a function вҖ” a named command you give some input to, inside parentheses. This one finds a square root:
sqrt(144)
[1] 12
(And yes, sqrt is plain R вҖ” youвҖҷre already learning the real thing. More on what a function actually is in a moment, once weвҖҷve used a second one.)
YouвҖҷll also see Terminal and Background Jobs tabs sharing this pane with the Console. TheyвҖҷre for more advanced use вҖ” the Terminal is a direct line to your computerвҖҷs own command prompt вҖ” and you can ignore them for now.


The Environment вҖ” what youвҖҷve loaded
The Console also runs commands that create things and keep them around. LetвҖҷs load a dataset, so the Environment has something to show. R ships with several built-in datasets for practice; one is mtcars (data on some classic car models). Copy it into a name of your own using RвҖҷs assignment operator, <-, which means вҖңput whatвҖҷs on the right into the name on the left.вҖқ Type this in the Console and press Enter вҖ” and watch the top-right of the screen as you do:
cars <- mtcars
Up in the top-right is the Environment pane вҖ” named for its main tab, Environment, open by default вҖ” and your new cars has just appeared there, described as 32 obs. of 11 variables. ThatвҖҷs its name and size, not the data themselves; the Environment lists what youвҖҷve loaded, it isnвҖҷt a window onto the data.
What cars actually is, in RвҖҷs terms, is a data frame: a table held in memory, with cases down the rows and variables across the columns. Two words are worth keeping apart. A dataset is the data at rest вҖ” a built-in like mtcars, or, later on, a data file on your disk. A data frame is the working copy R loads into memory for you to work on. So cars is a data frame вҖ” a copy of the built-in mtcars. That distinction has a practical side worth knowing from the start: when you change a data frame вҖ” recode a variable, say, or drop a few rows вҖ” R changes only the working copy in memory and leaves the original untouched, and your changes live in that working copy alone until you deliberately save them. With a built-in like cars thereвҖҷs no file of your own in play; but once youвҖҷre working with data youвҖҷve loaded from your own files, this is the thing to hold onto вҖ” nothing is written back to your file on disk until you choose to save. YouвҖҷre not limited to one, either вҖ” load or create several data frames and they all list here by name, and you pick which to work on simply by using its name.


Output / Files / Plots / Help вҖ” the bottom-right pane
The bottom-right pane gathers everyday tools as tabs: Files (the contents of your Project folder), Plots (any charts you make show up here), Packages (every package installed on your computer is listed here вҖ” jstats will appear once you install it, and ticking a packageвҖҷs checkbox loads it, the same job as library()), and Help (RвҖҷs built-in documentation). RStudioвҖҷs own name for it is the Output pane. YouвҖҷll dip into these constantly without thinking about them much.


The Source pane вҖ” where you write and keep code
Now the fourth pane. Open a new script вҖ” File вҶ’ New File вҶ’ R Script (or press Ctrl+Shift+N, or Cmd+Shift+N on a Mac) вҖ” and the Source pane opens in the top-left, nudging the Console down into the bottom-left. This is where you write code you want to keep.
ThatвҖҷs the one real difference between the two left-hand panes: the Console is for doing, the script is for keeping. Anything you type in the Console runs and then scrolls away вҖ” ideal for a quick, one-off job like a fast calculation. Code youвҖҷll want to re-run, revise, and share goes in a script: a plain text file (with a .R ending) that you save. Typing in a script doesnвҖҷt run anything on its own; when youвҖҷre ready, you send a line down to the Console to run it.
Try it. In your new script, type вҖ” or paste вҖ” the line below, then run it: put the cursor anywhere on the line and press Ctrl+Enter (Cmd+Enter on a Mac), or click Run at the top of the pane.
View(iris)
A small thing that catches beginners: RStudio runs the whole line the cursor is sitting on, but if you highlight text it runs exactly вҖ” and only вҖ” what youвҖҷve highlighted. So either leave the cursor on the line, or select the entire command; just donвҖҷt run with only part of a line highlighted, or R will try to run that fragment on its own, which usually fails. (R is pickier about this than many other applications.)
iris is another built-in dataset (measurements of flowers); being built in, itвҖҷs available by its own name without copying first. Note the capital V вҖ” View is one of a handful of base-R functions that breaks the usual all-lowercase habit, so view(iris) wonвҖҷt work. ThatвҖҷs a glimpse of a rule worth learning early: R is case-sensitive. Income and income are two different names to it, and a stray capital is one of the most common reasons something вҖңdoesnвҖҷt work.вҖқ (jstats keeps its own functions lowercase, in step with the base-R majority.)
Running the line opens a grid in the Source pane вҖ” a new tab alongside your script вҖ” with rows of flowers down the side and variables across the top. (Clicking a data frameвҖҷs name in the Environment opens the same read-only grid; View() just does it from code.) Notice one column, Species, holds group names вҖ” setosa, versicolor, virginica вҖ” instead of numbers: your first glimpse of a categorical variable, a column that sorts cases into groups. YouвҖҷll meet these properly later.

ThereвҖҷs one more idea worth pinning down before we move on, because nearly everything in R rests on it: the function. YouвҖҷve now used two вҖ” sqrt() and View() вҖ” and they share a shape: a function has a name, and you give it its input inside the parentheses that follow. sqrt(144) hands 144 to sqrt and gets 12 back; View(iris) hands iris to View and opens a data grid. That pattern вҖ” pick the function for the job, put your input in the parentheses, take the result вҖ” is how nearly all of R works. jstats, which youвҖҷll install next, is simply a collection of functions in this same form, named and tidied for the things social scientists do most. Once something(input) reads naturally to you, most R code does too.
YouвҖҷll meet mtcars and iris again and again in RвҖҷs help pages and other packagesвҖҷ examples вҖ” now you know what they are, so they wonвҖҷt catch you off guard. jstats brings its own example dataset too, community, which becomes available by name once you install and load the package; weвҖҷll use it from the Quick Start onward.






TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





A rough map if youвҖҷve mostly worked in commercial statistical software. The Console is like the output/results window where results appear (SPSSвҖҷs output viewer, StataвҖҷs Results window, SASвҖҷs Log and Output), and the Plots tab is where charts show up. The closest thing to a data view вҖ” SPSSвҖҷs Data View, StataвҖҷs Data Browser, SASвҖҷs table viewer вҖ” is the read-only grid that opens when you click a data frame in the Environment, or run View(); but the Environment pane itself is not that grid: it only lists whatвҖҷs loaded, by name and size. The Source pane is essentially the editor you already know under another name вҖ” the SPSS syntax editor, the Stata do-file editor, or the SAS program editor вҖ” a place to write commands, save them, and run them. If you write Stata or SAS code itвҖҷll feel almost identical; if youвҖҷve mostly clicked through SPSS menus, the syntax editor is the SPSS feature it most resembles, and the one habit most worth building.
One deeper difference in style: those tools are built around commands or procedures вҖ” SPSSвҖҷs FREQUENCIES, SASвҖҷs PROC MEANS, a Stata command, each followed by its options вҖ” whereas R is built around functions of the form name(input). Getting comfortable with that single, consistent shape (which the next pages lean on heavily) is the main adjustment.

title: jstats
section: Leaving notes in your code вҖ” the # symbol
text:
Leaving notes in your code вҖ” the # symbol
One small thing youвҖҷll see throughout the examples on this site, and in your own scripts before long. When R meets a # on a line, it ignores the # and everything after it, to the end of that line. That space is for you, not R вҖ” somewhere to leave a plain-language note about what a line does:
sqrt(144)    # find a square root
R runs the sqrt(144) part and pays no attention to the words after the #. A line that begins with # is all note and does nothing when run вҖ” purely a label for whoever reads the script later, including a future you whoвҖҷs forgotten the details. Notes like these are how a script stays readable, and youвҖҷll see them used throughout the code on these pages to explain each step as it happens.

title: jstats
section: Start each session with a clean slate
text:
Start each session with a clean slate
One quick setting is worth changing now. By default, when you quit RStudio it offers to save everything currently loaded вҖ” your whole Environment вҖ” and reload it next time. That sounds convenient, but it causes more confusion than it saves: old objects from a previous session linger, and you canвҖҷt easily tell what you meant to keep versus whatвҖҷs just left over. ItвҖҷs a classic source of вҖңwhy is this still here?вҖқ puzzlement.
The cleaner habit is to start every session empty and rebuild what you need by re-running your script вҖ” which is the dependable record of your work anyway. To set that up, go to Tools вҶ’ Global Options вҶ’ General, then:

uncheck Restore .RData into workspace at startup, and
set Save workspace to .RData on exit to Never.

Now each time you open RStudio you start fresh, and your script вҖ” not whatever happened to be in memory last time вҖ” is the source of truth.

With RStudio mapped out, the next page adds the jstats package itself.

title: jstats
text:
This is where you put jstats to work. WeвҖҷll take a quick tour of the package on a practice dataset that comes built in вҖ” describing and counting variables, screening a whole dataset in one pass, and finishing with a basic linear regression, the workhorse of the social sciences.
People come to jstats from very different starting points. Some readers have years of statistical experience and want to see what the package offers; others are early in a first statistics course. This tour is written for both: every command is easy to follow whatever your background, and if some of the output toward the end вҖ” the regression especially вҖ” involves statistics you havenвҖҷt been taught yet, thatвҖҷs fine and expected. The goal here isnвҖҷt to teach statistics; itвҖҷs to get you working with the package.
Every command below sits in a copy-and-paste box. If youвҖҷre following along with the companion video, you might keep this page open beside it so the commands stay close at hand вҖ” and whether you type or paste, enter each one exactly as it appears: R is case-sensitive, so capitalization has to match.

title: jstats
text:
Before you start
Two reminders from the earlier pages before we begin.
Open RStudio by opening your Project вҖ” the one you created for this practice work. From inside RStudio, thatвҖҷs File вҶ’ Open Project; or, in your computerвҖҷs file manager (File Explorer on Windows, Finder on a Mac), double-click the .Rproj file inside the ProjectвҖҷs folder. Working inside a Project keeps your files, your data, and your work together in one place. And remember which program youвҖҷre opening: RStudio, the window you work in вҖ” not the plain R application, which is only the engine underneath.
Load jstats for this session. R makes you load an installed package in each new session before you can use it. Installing jstats was the one-time step, and thatвҖҷs done; loading it is the every-session step, because a fresh session starts with nothing loaded. So the first command of the day is the library() command you met when you installed the package.

title: jstats
section: A script file, and loading jstats
text:
A script file, and loading jstats
WeвҖҷll give our commands a proper home. Rather than typing into the Console, weвҖҷll work in a script вҖ” a file where everything you do can be saved, ready to re-run, correct, or build on later. (You saw the division of labor in the orientation: the Console is for quick one-offs; a script is for work you keep.) Open one with File вҶ’ New File вҶ’ R Script; it appears in the Source pane, top-left.
The first line to type is the library command:
library(jstats)
Run it the way you practiced вҖ” cursor anywhere on the line, then Ctrl+Enter (Cmd+Enter on a Mac), or the Run button at the top of the pane. Down in the Console, jstats answers with a short startup message, and that message confirms the package is loaded for this session. (Typing one library line per session is no great burden вҖ” but if youвҖҷd rather have jstats load itself automatically whenever RStudio opens, Loading jstats automatically shows you how.)

title: jstats
section: Why jstats вҖ” a quick contrast
text:
Why jstats вҖ” a quick contrast
Now, with jstats loaded, hereвҖҷs what itвҖҷs for. The package ships with a practice dataset named community, and weвҖҷll use it for the whole tour (more on it shortly). Suppose you want to describe the ages of the people in the data вҖ” the average, how spread out they are, and so on. Base R вҖ” R as it comes, before any packages вҖ” can do this, and one common way takes three separate commands. You donвҖҷt need to learn these; weвҖҷre running them only for the comparison. Leaving a blank line between commands is optional вҖ” R ignores blank lines entirely, so you can use them to space a script for readability, or not:
mean(community$Age)

sd(community$Age)

range(community$Age)
[1] 40.65049
[1] 11.61955
[1] 18 71
You can run these one line at a time, or select all three and run them together вҖ” either way, R returns three answers, one beneath the other. Each line opens with the [1] you met in the orientation: thatвҖҷs R numbering its output, marking the position of the first value on the line вҖ” it isnвҖҷt part of the result. Beyond that, the answers are correct but bare: unlabeled numbers, one command per statistic. Describing five variables this way would mean fifteen lines. And some basic facts are still missing вҖ” how many respondents there are, and whether any ages are missing. HereвҖҷs the same job in jstats, with a function called jdesc (short for descriptives):
jdesc(community, Age)
Descriptive Statistics

Variable  Total  Non_missing  Min  Max   Mean     SD
--------  -----  -----------  ---  ---  -----  -----
Age         103          103   18   71  40.65  11.62

One command, one table вҖ” the total number of cases, how many are non-missing, the minimum and maximum, the mean, and the standard deviation, together and labeled, laid out the way youвҖҷd expect from commercial statistical software like SPSS or Stata. That, in a nutshell, is what jstats is for: taking the everyday work of data analysis and making it simple and readable. The rest of this tour builds on that idea.

title: jstats
text:
Meet community
That practice dataset, community, holds 103 simulated community-survey respondents, each with a handful of measures: income, education, age, a wellbeing score, a few yes/no items, a region of residence, and a five-item attitude scale. ItвҖҷs available the moment you load jstats вҖ” no file to open, nothing to import вҖ” which is what lets these examples, and the packageвҖҷs help-page examples, run without any setup. Bringing in and working with data of your own is covered in Loading, Saving, Converting Data.

title: jstats
section: Counting a categorical variable with jfreq
text:
Counting a categorical variable with jfreq
Next, the same pattern on a different job: counting the categories of a categorical variable. And it is the same pattern youвҖҷve already seen вҖ” choose the function, then give it your input inside the parentheses.
The jstats functions share a first letter, too: every one begins with j. ThatвҖҷs deliberate вҖ” the j sets the packageвҖҷs functions apart at a glance from base RвҖҷs and from other packagesвҖҷ вҖ” and it has a practical payoff. In RStudio, type j and press the Tab key, and the auto-complete list gathers the jstats functions together for you. (Not everything in that list is jstats вҖ” base R has a few functions of its own that begin with j вҖ” but most of what youвҖҷll see belongs to the package.)
This time the function is jfreq (short for frequencies), and the question is how our respondents are distributed across regions:
jfreq(community, Region)
Inside the parentheses, jfreq takes two things in order: the dataset (community) and the variable to count (Region). It prints a frequency table вҖ” the number of respondents in each region, alongside the percentages. (Note the capital R in Region: capitalization has to match, and to R a lowercase region would be a different name entirely.)
YouвҖҷll also see one more mark throughout these examples, from the orientation: the #. A # tells R to ignore the rest of the line вҖ” everything after it is a comment, a plain-language note for the human reader:
jfreq(community, Region)   # counts by region
Run that version and the table is identical вҖ” R carried out the command and passed straight over the note. Comments cost nothing, and they let your future self, and anyone else reading your script, see at a glance what each line is for.

title: jstats
text:
The examples just run
Every function comes with a help page, and each jstats help page ends with worked examples you can run yourself. LetвҖҷs open the one for the function we just used. A quick lookup like this is a natural job for the Console вҖ” the script is for work you keep, and this is a one-off вҖ” so down in the Console, type:
?jfreq
A ? followed by a functionвҖҷs name opens that functionвҖҷs help page in the bottom-right pane. Scroll to the bottom and youвҖҷll find the Examples section: copy one, paste it into the Console, and it runs immediately вҖ” because the data it uses are already loaded. No file to open, no path to set.
A fair word on this, especially if youвҖҷre coming from commercial statistical software: runnable help examples arenвҖҷt unique to R. Stata and SAS ship practice data too. WhatвҖҷs genuinely worth knowing is this. First, most of the packageвҖҷs examples are run automatically whenever itвҖҷs built and checked, so they donвҖҷt quietly fall out of date. Second, thereвҖҷs truly zero setup: community is simply there. Third, one source produces all three вҖ” the function, its help page, and its runnable example all come from the same place, so they canвҖҷt drift apart. The practical effect: when youвҖҷre learning a new function, you can always try it instantly on data that are already in front of you.

title: jstats
text:
Getting help on any function
You just used ?jfreq to open a help page. That works for every jstats function вҖ” and across R generally: type ? and the functionвҖҷs name. ThereвҖҷs also a shortcut: in your script, click a functionвҖҷs name and press F1 (Windows) or Fn+F1 (Mac). Either way the help page opens in the bottom-right pane, with a description, the available options, and worked examples you can run (they sit at the bottom, so scroll down).
And one more help page is worth knowing about вҖ” the packageвҖҷs own. Put the package name after the question mark this time:
?jstats
That opens the overall jstats help page. At the top is a description of the package вҖ” what it does and who itвҖҷs for. Below that, every current function is listed, grouped by purpose, each name a clickable link straight to that functionвҖҷs own page. Further down, the page describes the packageвҖҷs workflow conventions вҖ” how its functions are meant to string together, from the start of a session through to the finished results. When you canвҖҷt recall a functionвҖҷs name, or you want the full picture of whatвҖҷs available, this is the page to open.

title: jstats
text:
What a data frame is
A quick reminder of a term from the RStudio Orientation, because youвҖҷll meet it everywhere in R: a table of data like community is a data frame вҖ” cases in the rows, variables in the columns, held in memory and ready to work on. And recall the practical side: when you work with community, R is working on a copy held in memory вҖ” the one listed in your Environment pane вҖ” not the file on disk, so you can explore and change it freely, and nothing is written to disk until you deliberately save.






TipComing from SPSS or Stata?





If youвҖҷre arriving from SPSS or Stata, this is a genuine difference worth a pause. There, the dataset open in front of you is the file itself, and saving writes your changes straight back to it. R works on the in-memory copy instead, and leaves your original file untouched until you explicitly save. What you gain is the freedom to experiment without putting your data at risk; what it asks of you is remembering that nothing reaches the disk until you save it.

title: jstats
text:
Set a default dataset
You named community explicitly in your jfreq call. If youвҖҷll work with one dataset for a while, you can set it as the default and stop repeating its name. Do that with juse (short for use):
juse(community)
Now rerun the frequency table without naming the dataset:
jfreq(Region)
Same result вҖ” jstats fills in community for you. And juse changes nothing about the datasets themselves; it only marks which one jstats should reach for when you donвҖҷt say otherwise. (Your Environment can hold several datasets at once, each addressed by name, and you can switch the default whenever you like.)

title: jstats
text:
Describe the numeric variables
For numeric variables like age or income, you usually want the mean, the standard deviation, and the like rather than a full frequency table вҖ” and thatвҖҷs jdesc again. Because community is now the default, we name just the variables:
jdesc(Age, WellbeingScore, Income)
jstats returns the same table layout you saw at the start of the tour, now with one row per variable вҖ” three variables summarized in a single, readable table.

title: jstats
section: Screen the whole dataset at once
text:
Screen the whole dataset at once
Before any serious analysis, itвҖҷs good practice to survey the whole dataset вҖ” its ranges, possible outliers, skewed distributions, missing values, and what kind each variable seems to be. jscreen (short for screen) folds that whole first look into one call:
jscreen()
With the juse default set, a bare jscreen() screens every variable in community and gives you a fast, organized read on the dataвҖҷs health. WeвҖҷre keeping this light here вҖ” jscreen can report a good deal more, and its help page and the books treat it in full depth вҖ” but even the quick look is a habit worth building: analysis goes better when you know what your data are before you start.

title: jstats
text:
Your first analysis: a regression
The last stop is a basic linear regression, the workhorse of the social sciences. If youвҖҷve run regressions for years, watch how little typing this takes вҖ” and notice that the printed results go well beyond base RвҖҷs. If youвҖҷre meeting regression for the first time, hereвҖҷs the one-sentence version: a regression asks whether one variable tends to rise or fall as another changes, and puts a number both on the strength of that relationship and on how confidently it can be told apart from chance. WeвҖҷll ask whether age predicts wellbeing among our respondents.
The function is jlm (short for linear model, the formal name for this kind of analysis). If youвҖҷre an experienced R user, youвҖҷll recognize it as a close cousin of base RвҖҷs own lm вҖ” the resemblance is deliberate: jstats stays near base R wherever it can, so what you practice here carries over. HereвҖҷs the command in its simplest form:
jlm(WellbeingScore ~ Age)






TipIf youвҖҷre new to statistics





A regression asks a simple question: as one thing changes, does another tend to change along with it, and by how much? Here weвҖҷre asking whether wellbeing tends to rise (or fall) as age goes up. The output puts a number on that tendency, and on how confident we can be that itвҖҷs a real pattern rather than chance.



One new piece of notation sits in the middle: the wavy character, called a tilde. The tilde is how R writes a model, and the rule is simple вҖ” the outcome (the variable you want to explain, here the wellbeing score) sits on its left, and the predictor (the variable doing the explaining, here age) sits on its right. So read WellbeingScore ~ Age as вҖңwellbeing, explained by age.вҖқ (We didnвҖҷt name the dataset: juse(community) is still in effect, and jstats notes which data frame it used at the top of the output.)
Now hereвҖҷs a second way to run the same analysis вҖ” one youвҖҷll see everywhere in R:
Results <- jlm(WellbeingScore ~ Age)
That arrow on the left вҖ” typed as a less-than sign followed by a hyphen вҖ” is RвҖҷs assignment operator. It stores whatever is produced on its right under the name on its left: here, the complete results of the regression, saved under the name Results. That name is our own choice, by the way вҖ” thereвҖҷs nothing special about the word Results; we could have called it almost anything. In base R this is how results are generally kept, and youвҖҷll see the arrow constantly. In jstats itвҖҷs optional: you use it when you want to keep an analysisвҖҷs results for later work вҖ” say, passing Results to another function that formats them into a polished table for a report or article вҖ” without running the regression again.
And notice what happened in the Console when that second form ran: the full results printed again. ThatвҖҷs the default behavior of every jstats analysis function вҖ” it prints its results and hands you the stored copy, so the arrow costs you nothing.
вҖңDefaultвҖқ is worth dwelling on, because it points to something useful as you go further: most jstats functions accept options. Open any functionвҖҷs help page and youвҖҷll find them вҖ” options that adjust what the function does, and options that adjust what appears in the output (the number of digits shown, how variable and value labels appear, which informational notes print, and more). You can set these per command, or once for the whole session through two functions, joptions and joutput. We wonвҖҷt tour them here; just know the controls are there, and the help pages show the way.

title: jstats
text:
Read the output
Take a moment to look over what jstats printed. If regression is familiar territory, youвҖҷll find the essentials of a standard analysis laid out in this single output вҖ” the coefficients, their significance tests, the model-fit statistics вҖ” with nothing further to request or assemble. If regression is new to you, donвҖҷt try to decode the table today: learning what each number means is the business of an introductory statistics course, and of the books that accompany this package, one careful piece at a time. For now the point is simpler вҖ” one short command produced a complete, readable analysis, and a saved copy sits in Results whenever youвҖҷre ready to use it.

title: jstats
text:
What youвҖҷve done
In one short script, you described a variable, counted a categorical one, summarized several variables at once, screened an entire dataset, and ran a linear regression. Each task had its own function, but every function took the same form вҖ” and once juse set the default, none of them needed the dataset retyped, or the dollar-sign construction (community$Age) you saw in the base R lines at the start. At the same time, that form stays close enough to base R that what youвҖҷve practiced transfers вҖ” to base R itself, and to other packagesвҖҷ functions, whenever your work calls for them.
Your script can be saved, too, so todayвҖҷs commands are there to reopen and build on in a future session. Saving script files вҖ” and where they live inside your Project вҖ” is covered in a later guide.






NoteWhere to go next



The natural next step is bringing in data of your own вҖ” your own files, and the realities that come with them, like respondents who declined to answer a question (income, in community, is one such variable). Handling missing values like those is exactly what the next part is about: Loading, Saving, Converting Data.

text:
These are the two pieces everything else sits on: R (the engine that does the statistics) and RStudio (the friendlier window you drive it from). Both are free. Install R first, then RStudio on top of it.
You only do this once per computer. If you need to set up a second machine later, this page is the one to come back to.

  
    
  

From the video вҖ” download R from CRAN at https://cran.r-project.org, and RStudio Desktop from https://docs.posit.co/ide/user/#rstudio-ide-oss-downloads. Both are walked through in the written steps just below.






NoteSeeing security warnings?



A blue вҖңWindows protected your PCвҖқ box, or a prompt about an unrecognized publisher, is normal for brand-new software вҖ” click More info, then Run anyway. Launch the downloaded installer from your Downloads folder in File Explorer rather than from the browserвҖҷs download list: on some university- or employer-managed computers, the browser route can show the blue box with no вҖңMore infoвҖқ link and no way past it, while the Downloads-folder method works everywhere. The Troubleshooting page walks through each box вҖ” with a short note you can pass to your IT department if youвҖҷre on a managed computer.

title: jstats
text:
Install R
Go to CRAN вҖ” the official archive of R, at https://cran.r-project.org вҖ” choose the download for your system (Windows, Mac, or Linux), and run the installer with its default settings. ThatвҖҷs it; you wonвҖҷt open R directly вҖ” RStudio will.
On a Mac, one extra choice matters. The Mac download page offers two installers вҖ” one for Apple silicon Macs (the newer M1/M2/M3/M4 chips) and one for older Intel Macs вҖ” and you want the one that matches your computer. To check which you have, open the Apple menu (top-left of the screen) and choose About This Mac: a line reading Chip: Apple MвҖҰ means Apple silicon, while Processor: вҖҰIntelвҖҰ means Intel. Pick the matching installer вҖ” its filename ends in arm64 for Apple silicon and x86_64 for Intel. A newer Mac will run the Intel version through AppleвҖҷs built-in translation, so grabbing the wrong one isnвҖҷt a disaster вҖ” but the matching build runs faster and avoids a class of confusing errors that can show up later when you install packages. (Windows has a single installer, so thereвҖҷs no equivalent choice to make there.)
crumbs: Step 2 вҖ” Install R and RStudio

title: jstats
text:
Install RStudio
Download RStudio Desktop from https://docs.posit.co/ide/user/#rstudio-ide-oss-downloads and install it. (Install R first; RStudio needs it.) On a Mac, the download is a disk image (.dmg): open it, then drag the RStudio icon into your Applications folder. From now on, RStudio is the program you open to work.
The download page also mentions paid products вҖ” those are enterprise tools aimed at large organizations, not something youвҖҷre missing out on. The free RStudio Desktop is the full program, and itвҖҷs the one youвҖҷll open every time you work.

title: jstats
text:
RStudioвҖҷs own getting-started guide
You may come across RStudioвҖҷs own Get Started guide while poking around. ItвҖҷs well made, but itвҖҷs built around a different set of tools вҖ” the tidyverse family of packages (especially ggplot2 for graphs) вҖ” rather than jstats, so the commands it teaches arenвҖҷt the ones these guides use. It also describes the RStudio window as showing four panes the moment you open it; in practice youвҖҷll see three at first, with the fourth (the top-left editor) appearing once you open a script or view a dataset вҖ” which is how the next page describes it, to match what youвҖҷll actually see.
It overlaps a good deal with our own orientation but follows a different path, so for the jstats route the simplest thing is to skip it and keep going here. The next page is that orientation вҖ” a quick tour of RStudio (the panes, the console, and how your data looks once itвҖҷs loaded) вҖ” before you add the jstats package itself.

title: jstats
text:
New here?
The Get Started guide takes you from nothing installed to your first real statistical result вҖ” an actual regression вҖ” in about an hour, with no prior R experience needed. YouвҖҷll do a little setup once, then spend your time getting results.

NoteComing soon



Full details, sample chapters, and where to buy will live here.


Two books are planned:

Statistics in R, Made Accessible вҖ” for readers meeting statistics and R at the same time. Warm and patient, assuming no prior knowledge of either, building carefully from the ground up.
From SPSS and Stata to R вҖ” for researchers who already know the statistics and want a clear path into R with jstats: function options, design rationale, and migration patterns from commercial software.

The free Get Started guide and the How-Tos section are a reduced version of the booksвҖҷ material; the books are the deeper, complete treatment.
jstats is more than a single package. Around it is a growing set of free online guides, this book series, a video channel, and ready-to-use teaching datasets. It follows a long tradition of teaching-focused statistics packages that began in the classroom and grew into something larger вҖ” Danielle NavarroвҖҷs lsr, the companion to her widely used Learning Statistics with R, is one well-loved example вҖ” and the aim here is the same.
