title: jstats
text:
This page is a hands-on run-through of moving data in and out of jstats: loading files, saving your work back out, and converting between formats.






NoteBefore you start





To follow along youвҖҷll need:

R, RStudio, and jstats installed. If theyвҖҷre not, work through Get Started, Steps 1-5 first and come back.
An RStudio Project open, so your working directory and files are already set up.
A new, empty R script open (File > New File > R Script). YouвҖҷll copy each numbered block into it, one at a time, running each before moving on.










NoteHow to Use this Page





The blocks on this page build one script. Paste each block into the same R script file as you reach it, in order вҖ” by the end, the whole file runs cleanly from top to bottom, except in the few places where a comment says an error is deliberate (so you can see what one looks like).
A few mechanics worth knowing:

Every block starts with a dashed comment header (## ---- 1. ... ----). It marks where one block ends and the next begins in your growing script вҖ” and RStudio reads those headers as sections, so they appear in the scriptвҖҷs outline for easy jumping around.
Experiment freely as you go вҖ” change a number, rerun a line, poke at things. Building the script yourself is the point.
We recommend working through the blocks one at a time first вҖ“ you learn far more by building the script yourself and watching each piece run than by reading a finished file. Once youвҖҷve done that (or if youвҖҷd rather grab everything at once), the complete, clean script вҖ“ without your experiments, and finished so it runs top to bottom вҖ“ is available to download at the bottom of the page.

This box is the same on every hands-on page in this group, so once youвҖҷve read it here, you can skip it elsewhere.









TipNew to R?





You donвҖҷt need to memorize any of this yet. Right now the goal is simply to run each line and watch what happens вҖ“ the ideas settle in as you go. A few things R doesnвҖҷt care about, so you donвҖҷt have to either: which line numbers your code lands on, and how many blank lines sit between commands вҖ“ paste each block anywhere below your previous code and it all works the same. Lines starting with # are comments: notes for humans that R skips entirely (extra hash marks, like ##, are still just a comment). And running a line a second time is almost always harmless вҖ“ youвҖҷll simply get the same result again. If a line doesnвҖҷt work, check that jstats loaded without errors, then try it again.









NoteReset your session





Experimenting means the occasional wrong turn вҖ“ a setting changed, the practice dataset overwritten, output that suddenly looks different. When something feels off and youвҖҷd like a clean footing without restarting RStudio, run these two lines in the Console:
## Put jstats' output settings back to their defaults
joutput("standard")   # output level, and decimal places back to 3

## Point saving and loading back at your working directory
joptions(data.dir = "")
That resets jstats itself. To clear away objects you created while following along вҖ“ data frames like MyData_r, or a value like MyObject вҖ“ the simplest way is the little broom icon at the top of the Environment pane (upper-right): one click sweeps the pane clean. It clears everything in the Environment, so use it when youвҖҷre between run-throughs, not in the middle of one. (You can also remove a single object by name, e.g.В rm(MyData_r).) The built-in practice data comes right back with jload("community") whenever you want it.
This box is the same on every hands-on page in this group.





WeвҖҷll start by loading the package and one of its built-in practice datasets.
## ---- 1. Load the package and a dataset ---------------------------------

## Load the jstats library (needed once per R session)
library(jstats)

## Load the package dataset called community
## It's loaded as a data frame into the Environment
jload("community")






NoteWhat you should see






The command lines are shown; the ## comment lines arenвҖҷt вҖ“ running a comment just echoes it back and does nothing.


Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminalГ—Background JobsГ—> library(jstats)
> jload("community")
Loaded the jstats example dataset 'community'.
5 variables have user-defined missing values:
  Income: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Education: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Smoker: -99 ["Refused"]
  Environment1: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Environment3: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
These codes are excluded as missing in jstats analyses. For better base R compatibility, convert them:
  jconvert(community, to = "stata")  - retains missing-value codes, base R compatible (recommended)
  jconvert(community, to = "baseR")  - converts to plain NA and removes missing-value codes
> |

Environment (upper-right pane)


EnvironmentHistoryConnectionsTutorial


Data


community103 obs. of 15 variables












NoteWhatвҖҷs the point?





library(jstats) switches the package on for the current session вҖ“ and once per session is all it takes. If youвҖҷve just come from the Quick Start page, jstats may well be loaded already; running the line again is harmless (it simply does nothing the second time). You can check at any point in RStudioвҖҷs Packages tab (lower-right pane): the checkbox beside jstats is ticked while the package is loaded. On a fresh load, jstats also prints a short version message in the Console; if it was already loaded, the line stays quiet. And if youвҖҷd rather never type the line again, R can run it for you at the start of every session вҖ“ the Loading jstats automatically page shows how.









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





For a dataset that ships inside a package, base R has one too: data(community) loads it. The difference shows up beyond package data: data() only reaches datasets bundled in packages, while jload() is one loader for everything on this page вҖ“ package datasets, your saved .rds files, and SPSS, Stata, SAS, Excel, and CSV files вҖ“ with the same one-line call.






Anything you create in R is stored as an object, and objects live in the Global Environment вҖ“ the workspace RStudio shows you in the Environment tab of the upper-right pane. Here we make two objects of our own, and open a second dataset alongside the first.
## ---- 2. Objects, the assignment operator, and the Global Environment ----

## "Objects" other than data frames can go into the Environment
## The "Assignment Operator" (less-than followed by a dash) is a key Base R fundamental
## Take the thing on the right and assign it to what's on the left
## Take the Region variable from the community data frame and assign it to a new object
## The new object we'll call "MyObject"
MyObject <- community$Region ## (Base R code)

## The name on the left is entirely yours to choose, and the thing on the
## right can be anything at all -- here, just the number 5
x <- 5

## You are not limited to one dataset at a time
## Load a second package dataset, clinic, alongside community
jload("clinic")






NoteWhat you should see






Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminalГ—Background JobsГ—> MyObject <- community$Region
> x <- 5
> jload("clinic")

Suspected missing-value codes detected:
  MoodRating:  -99 (4), -98 (3)
  Anxiety2:    -99 (3), -98 (3)

These codes are not formally defined, so they are not treated as missing.
Declare them as missing if they are; leave as-is if real.

# To declare one variable's codes as missing:
  clinic <- jdeclare_udm(clinic, MoodRating, codes = c(-99, -98))
# To label them at the same time (example only):
#   clinic <- jdeclare_udm(clinic, MoodRating, codes = c("label1" = -99, "label2" = -98))
Loaded the jstats example dataset 'clinic'.
4 variables have user-defined missing values:
  Stress: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  SleepHours: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Medication: -99 ["Refused"]
  Anxiety4: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
These codes are excluded as missing in jstats analyses. For better base R compatibility, convert them:
  jconvert(clinic, to = "stata")  - retains missing-value codes, base R compatible (recommended)
  jconvert(clinic, to = "baseR")  - converts to plain NA and removes missing-value codes
> |

Environment (upper-right pane)


EnvironmentHistoryConnectionsTutorial


Data


clinic70 obs. of 16 variables


community103 obs. of 15 variables


Values


MyObjectinteger+lbl [1:103] 1, 3, 1, 1, 1, 2, 1, 4, 1, 2вҖҰ


x5












TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





The Global Environment has no real SPSS counterpart, and that is the most useful thing to know about it. In SPSS everything lives inside a dataset: COMPUTE x = 5. does not create an object called x вҖ“ it adds a column of 5s, one value per case, to whatever dataset is active. There is nowhere for a single value, or a single variable, to sit on its own. (SPSS macros come closest, and hardly anyone uses them.) In R the Environment is exactly that place: a workspace holding objects of any shape, and a data frame is simply one of the shapes.
SPSS can have several datasets open too вҖ“ but each opens in its own Data Editor window, and switching the active one takes a syntax chain most users never bother with:
GET FILE='community.sav'.
DATASET NAME community.
GET FILE='clinic.sav'.
DATASET NAME clinic.
DATASET ACTIVATE community.
In R there are no windows to juggle: the Environment holds several data frames side by side, and every command simply names the one it uses вҖ“ the $ in community$Region means вҖңthe Region column, in the community dataset.вҖқ






Objects you no longer need can be removed, and there are two ways to do it. rm() takes out exactly what you name and leaves everything else alone вҖ“ which is what you want when one object is wrong and the others are fine. The broom in the Environment paneвҖҷs toolbar takes out everything, in one click; RStudio asks you to confirm before it does. The code below uses rm(); the red arrow in the box points out the broom.
One thing to expect: rm() says nothing at all when it works. Silence is success вҖ“ the Environment pane is where you check.
## ---- 3. Cleaning up ----------------------------------------------------

## Remove MyObject from the Environment
## Note that this is a Base R function (j is not the first letter)
rm(MyObject)

## Remove x as well
rm(x)

## Remove the community data frame
rm(community)

## Remove the clinic data frame
rm(clinic)






NoteWhat you should see






Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminalГ—Background JobsГ—> rm(MyObject)
> rm(x)
> rm(community)
> rm(clinic)
> |

Environment (upper-right pane)


EnvironmentHistoryConnectionsTutorial


Environment is empty




The red arrow points to the broom вҖ“ RStudio calls it Clear objects from the workspace. It empties the Global Environment in one go, which is the button form of the rm(list = ls()) line you will meet later on this page.










TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





When you have finished with a dataset in SPSS you close its Data Editor window, or run DATASET CLOSE clinic. вҖ“ rm() is the R counterpart. The difference is that the Environment holds more than datasets, so rm() removes any object you name, whatever its shape.






jstats can show more or less detail. The output level controls how much appears вҖ“ so with the Environment empty, load community again and watch it say less.
## ---- 4. How much output you see ----------------------------------------

## The output level is set to "standard" by default
## After you learn more about jstats (and R) you might not want all of that output
joutput("minimal")

## With minimal output, you get less information
jload("community")

## Set the output level back to standard
joutput("standard")






NoteWhat you should see






Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminalГ—Background JobsГ—> joutput("minimal")
Output Settings
Level: minimal
  effect.size: OFF
  regression.ci: OFF
  means.ci: OFF
  levene: OFF
  posthoc: OFF
  diagnostics: OFF
  case.processing: OFF
  case.processing.detail: NONE
  variable.id: NAMES
  value.id: LABELS
  ref.categories: OFF
  udm.notice: OFF
  digits: 3

> jload("community")
Loaded the jstats example dataset 'community'.
> joutput("standard")
Output Settings
Level: standard
  effect.size: ON
  regression.ci: OFF
  means.ci: ON
  levene: OFF
  posthoc: OFF
  diagnostics: OFF
  case.processing: AUTO
  case.processing.detail: TOTALS
  variable.id: NAMES
  value.id: BOTH
  ref.categories: ON
  udm.notice: ON
  digits: 3

> |

Environment (upper-right pane)


EnvironmentHistoryConnectionsTutorial


Data


community103 obs. of 15 variables












NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





There isnвҖҷt one. Base R has no output-level setting вҖ“ each function prints whatever it prints, and how much you see varies with each package authorвҖҷs choices. joutput() is a single, jstats-wide control.






Most functions need something inside the parentheses, but a few work on their own. Every function also has a help page.
## ---- 5. Functions with no arguments, and getting help ------------------

## A few functions work correctly without input between the parentheses, but most require input
jload()  # This will cause an error with a message about how you might fix the error

## But this one will show you the current output settings for jstats
joutput()

## Here's a Base R function that works without arguments/input
## List the objects/data frames in the Environment
ls()

## Here's another one from Base R
## A working directory is the "path" to where your project is stored on your computer
## If you're not working in a project, you have to set this yourself (finicky)
getwd()

## The technical name for a function's inputs are "Arguments"
## View help files describing necessary and optional Arguments by running...
?jload

## The same ? help works for base R functions and functions from any installed package
?ls

## And this one opens the package overview: every jstats function, grouped by purpose
?jstats






NoteAside: how R writes file paths





A path is a folderвҖҷs address on your computer вҖ“ the folders you would click through, written out on one line. Three spelling quirks are worth thirty seconds each, because you will meet all three on this page.
The ~ shorthand. Look at the top of your Console: RStudio shows your working directory with a squiggle, something like ~/your-working-directory/. The ~ is shorthand for your home folder (on Windows, usually your Documents folder). getwd() prints that same address written out in full вҖ“ beginning, on Windows, with a drive letter such as C: or E: that names the disk. Short spelling in the Console header, long spelling from getwd(). Same place.
You have met that squiggle before, on the Quick Start page, doing a completely different job вҖ“ writing a model, as in jt(Optimism ~ Gender). In a file path it has nothing to do with models. Same character, two unrelated jobs.
The slashes. R writes paths with forward slashes (/) on every kind of computer. Windows itself writes the same address with backslashes: File Explorer shows E:\00 R Projects, R shows E:/00 R Projects. Same folder, two spellings. When you type a path in R, use /.
So: ~ means home, / goes between folders, and getwd() tells you the full address of where you are working.
And if all of that felt like a lot, donвҖҷt worry. You will not have to type a path anywhere on this page. As long as you work inside an RStudio Project вҖ“ which is what Before you start asks of you вҖ“ jstats handles the addresses for you. In a moment you will name a folder once, with joptions(data.dir = "Data"), and jstats creates it inside your project; from then on you load and save with plain file names like "MyData_r.rds", and jstats works out where they belong. Paths are worth recognizing when a message prints one. They are not something you need to write.









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





? is base R вҖ“ ?jload, ?ls, and ?mean all work the same way, for base functions and for functions from any installed package. (In RStudio, putting the cursor on a function name and pressing F1 does the same thing. On Macs the key varies from machine to machine вҖ“ F1 on some, fn + F1 on others, and on some Macs the function keys must first be enabled in System Settings.) What a base R setup doesnвҖҷt have is a grouped index: a typical workflow spans functions scattered across many packages, each with its own alphabetical help listing. ?jstats shows the whole toolkit on one page, organized by purpose вҖ“ one advantage of the functions traveling together as a package.






When you load a dataset thatвҖҷs already open, jstats checks with you before overwriting it вҖ“ watch for the y/n prompt in the Console window.
## ---- 6. The overwrite prompt -------------------------------------------

## The output you get in the Console Pane can include a confirmation prompt
## Here, jstats will not overwrite community unless you give it permission
jload("community") # Look at the y/n prompt in the console

## Set an optional overwrite argument/input to overwrite without the prompt
jload("community", overwrite = TRUE)






NoteWhatвҖҷs the point?





The overwrite argument is here to show you two things:

What an argument looks like. Options go inside the parentheses as name = value вҖ“ here, overwrite = TRUE.
Prompts can be overridden. The few times jstats asks for confirmation, the prompt appears in the Console вҖ“ and each one can be switched off with the right argument. That matters when code runs automatically, with nobody at the keyboard to answer: it happens in several places behind this website, and in the finished script you can download at the bottom of this page.










NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





Base R doesnвҖҷt ask. Loading or assigning over an existing object replaces it silently вҖ“ no prompt, no warning, and the old copy is gone. The confirmation prompt is a jstats safeguard.






LetвҖҷs save your work вҖ“ first with no folder specified, to see where files go by default, and then to a Data folder of your own.
## ---- 7. Saving to a Data folder ----------------------------------------

## Use the assignment operator to copy community to a new data frame called MyData
MyData <- community

## Save it with no folder specified -- where does the file go?
## The console message shows the full path: it landed in your project's
## working directory, the default location
## It's the same path the Base R getwd() command showed you earlier
jsave(MyData, "WhereDidIGo.rds")

## Delete that test file
## file.remove() is base R -- the file-on-disk equivalent of the rm()
## you used for data frames. It replies with a cryptic [1] TRUE
file.remove("WhereDidIGo.rds")

## Now name a Data folder of your own
## jstats will create this folder upon first save
## Or you can create it manually
joptions(data.dir = "Data")

## Save the dataset to the Data folder and call it MyData_r.rds
## Specify R native format with the .rds extension
## The Data folder is created now, on this first save
jsave(MyData, "MyData_r.rds")






NoteWhat you should see






Where these messages read ~/your-working-directory/, your own screen shows the real name of your project folder вҖ“ the same one the Console header names, just above.


Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminalГ—Background JobsГ—> MyData <- community
> jsave(MyData, "WhereDidIGo.rds")
Saved MyData to ~/your-working-directory/WhereDidIGo.rds (R native format; 103 cases, 15 variables)
> file.remove("WhereDidIGo.rds")
[1] TRUE
> joptions(data.dir = "Data")
Options Settings
User-defined missing values (UDMs) convention: None selected
UDM convention codes: -99, -98, -97
Data folder: Data (will be created on first save)
Correlation layout: wide

> jsave(MyData, "MyData_r.rds")
Created 'Data' folder in working directory.
Saved MyData to ~/your-working-directory/Data/MyData_r.rds (R native format; 103 cases, 15 variables)
> |









NoteWhatвҖҷs the point?





Why set a data folder at all? Tidiness now, and a payoff later. With data.dir set, every save and load on this page knows where to look. And thatвҖҷs the habit to keep: put joptions(data.dir = "Data") near the top of your own scripts, then drop a dataset of yours into that folder and jload("YourFile.sav") opens it by name alone. If youвҖҷre not sure where the folder is on your computer, the jsave message has already told you вҖ“ it prints the full path each time, ready to paste into File Explorer (Windows) or find in Finder (Mac).









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





Saving to the working directory by default is base RвҖҷs behavior too вҖ“ saveRDS(MyData, "MyData_r.rds") puts the file in the same place; jstats just tells you, in full, where it went. To use a folder, base R wonвҖҷt create it for you (thatвҖҷs dir.create("Data") first), and the folder name gets typed into every path from then on вҖ“ with any subfolders along for the ride, e.g. saveRDS(MyData, "Data/CommunityStudy/Wave1/MyData_r.rds"). joptions(data.dir = ...) sets the location once, and every jsave() and jload() after that already knows where to look.
And note what file.remove() didnвҖҷt do: ask. Like the silent overwrite in block 6, base R deletes with no confirmation вҖ“ mix up a filename and a real data file is gone. jstatsвҖҷs prompts exist precisely because of edges like that.






You have just saved MyData to a file on disk. Now remove it вҖ“ and community вҖ“ from the Environment. Before you run the block, make a prediction: is the file you just saved gone as well?
## ---- 8. Gone from the Environment --------------------------------------

## Remove both data frames from the Environment
## What happens to the file you just saved? The next block finds out
rm(MyData)
rm(community)






NoteWhat you should see






Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminalГ—Background JobsГ—> rm(MyData)
> rm(community)
> |

Environment (upper-right pane)


EnvironmentHistoryConnectionsTutorial


Environment is empty









There it is. The Environment is temporary; the file on disk is not. Load it straight back вҖ“ then send it out to other formats, where one wrinkle around missing values needs handling, and jstats handles it for you.
## ---- 9. Getting it back, and saving across formats ---------------------

## The file survived: jload() brings the saved dataset straight back
jload("MyData_r.rds")

## The original community dataset carries SPSS-style missing values
## So it saves to SPSS format (.sav) without a problem
jsave(MyData_r, "MyData_spss.sav")

## But the SPSS-style missing-value codes need to be converted
## if you want to save to a format other than R or SPSS
jsave(MyData_r, "MyData_stata.dta")  # This will draw an error

## Convert to Stata format (.dta)
MyData_stata <- jconvert(MyData_r, to = "stata")
jsave(MyData_stata, "MyData_stata.dta")  # Now no error






NoteWhat you should see






Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminalГ—Background JobsГ—> jload("MyData_r.rds")
Reading from ~/your-working-directory/Data
Loaded MyData_r (R native format; 103 cases, 15 variables)
5 variables have user-defined missing values:
  Income: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Education: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Smoker: -99 ["Refused"]
  Environment1: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Environment3: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
These codes are excluded as missing in jstats analyses. For better base R compatibility, convert them:
  jconvert(MyData_r, to = "stata")  - retains missing-value codes, base R compatible (recommended)
  jconvert(MyData_r, to = "baseR")  - converts to plain NA and removes missing-value codes
> jsave(MyData_r, "MyData_spss.sav")
Saved MyData_r to ~/your-working-directory/Data/MyData_spss.sav (SPSS format; 103 cases, 15 variables)
> jsave(MyData_r, "MyData_stata.dta")
Error: jsave(): 5 variables carry SPSS-style missing values, incompatible with the .dta format.

  Income, Education, Smoker, Environment1, Environment3
Before saving to Stata format, convert with:
  MyData_r <- jconvert(MyData_r, to = "stata")
> MyData_stata <- jconvert(MyData_r, to = "stata")
Converted to Stata-style missing values in 5 variables:
  Income        (-99 "Refused" -> .a, -98 "Don't know" -> .b)
  Education     (-99 "Refused" -> .a, -98 "Don't know" -> .b)
  Smoker        (-99 "Refused" -> .a)
  Environment1  (-99 "Refused" -> .a, -98 "Don't know" -> .b)
  Environment3  (-99 "Refused" -> .a, -98 "Don't know" -> .b)

Assign the result to keep the conversion:
  MyData_r <- jconvert(MyData_r, ...)

To keep it across sessions, save the data frame:
  jsave(MyData_r, "MyData_r.rds")
> jsave(MyData_stata, "MyData_stata.dta")
Saved MyData_stata to ~/your-working-directory/Data/MyData_stata.dta (Stata format; 103 cases, 15 variables)
> |

Environment (upper-right pane)


EnvironmentHistoryConnectionsTutorial


Data


MyData_r103 obs. of 15 variables


MyData_stata103 obs. of 15 variables












TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





In SPSS, user-missing values are declared in Variable View and travel with the file. When you Save As a different format, those definitions donвҖҷt always carry across cleanly. jstats keeps the equivalent information with your data, and when a format needs it in a different form, jconvert() does the translation вҖ“ which is why saving straight to Stata format above stops and asks you to convert first.









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





Base R alone canвҖҷt write SPSS or Stata files. YouвҖҷd install and load the haven package and call haven::write_sav() or haven::write_dta() yourself вҖ“ and haven is exactly the proven library jstats uses under the hood, so nothing here reinvents the wheel. What jstats spares you is the setup and the sharp edge: loading an extra package in every script, learning its separate function set, and handling the missing-value translation by hand вҖ“ haven refuses SPSS-style missing codes headed into Stata format, just as you saw above, and converting them yourself is genuinely fiddly. jconvert() is that translation, done for you.
Two conversion habits are worth knowing even if youвҖҷre an experienced R user.






Load the saved files back and see how each format keeps its missing-value codes.
## ---- 10. Reloading, and how missing codes travel -----------------------

## You removed data frames one at a time earlier; this clears them all at once
## It is the code equivalent of the broom button in the Environment pane

One more control before the final check: how many decimal places jstats shows. The default setting is 3, the maximum is 7 вҖ“ and for what comes next, we want all 7. (One exception: p-values keep their own fixed three-decimal convention and ignore this setting.)
## ---- 11. Turning up the digits -----------------------------------------

## By default, jstats shows up to 3 decimal places
jdesc(MyData_r, Age)   # see the Age mean below -- the callouts explain the decimals

## Turn the display up to the maximum of 7 decimal places
joutput(digits = 7)
jdesc(MyData_r, Age)   # the same mean, now with more decimals shown

TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





SPSS output shows a fixed number of decimals, padding with zeros to fill the width вҖ“ a mean of 40.65 appears as 40.650 in a three-decimal table. jstats goes the other way: it never prints trailing zeros, so digits sets a maximum, and each column shows only the decimals it actually needs. ThatвҖҷs why the mean above reads 40.65 at the default setting of 3 вҖ“ its third decimal happens to be a zero. And if a statistic lands exactly on a round value, it displays short even at digits = 7: thatвҖҷs trimming, not truncation or a stuck setting.









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





The closest is options(digits = ...), and itвҖҷs a different animal: it sets significant digits, globally, for nearly everything R prints. joutput(digits = ...) means decimal places, and it changes only jstats output.






Those three data frames each made a round trip through a different file format. If the trips were truly lossless, the same statistics should come back from every one of them вҖ“ right down to the seventh decimal.
## ---- 12. The proof that nothing was lost -------------------------------

## Ask for the mean of Age from each of the three reloaded data frames
jdesc(MyData_r, Age)
jdesc(MyData_spss, Age)
jdesc(MyData_stata, Age)
## All three report the identical mean, to the last decimal:
## same data, three file formats, nothing lost along the way

## And one base R caution, shown live (the box below explains):
## Income carries declared missing-value codes (-99, -98)
jdesc(MyData_spss, Income)   # the correct mean -- jdesc leaves the codes out
## Base R gives a bare, unlabeled number, so extra work is needed just to
## make it visible: cat() adds a label, and the "\n"s add blank lines to
## set it apart. Run line by line (interactively), a plain mean() prints
## only a cryptic [1] number; run with Source, it wouldn't appear at all
cat("\n\nBase R mean of Income:", mean(MyData_spss$Income), "\n\n\n")  # a poisoned mean

## Set the display back to the default before you go
joutput(digits = 3)

## Put the data folder setting back to its default (the working directory) too
## Passing "" clears it (data.dir = NULL would leave it unchanged)
## A finished script leaves your session exactly as it found it
joptions(data.dir = "")

TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





jdesc() is the counterpart of SPSSвҖҷs Analyze > Descriptive Statistics > Descriptives вҖ“ the same N, minimum, maximum, mean, and standard deviation youвҖҷd read from that output table. And the check above may feel familiar from practice: reopening a dataset thatвҖҷs been through another format, and confirming the descriptives still match.
The matching numbers are only half the story вҖ“ the missing-value declarations made the round trip too, which is arguably the bigger deal. You saw it back in block 10: EducationвҖҷs codes come through meaning the same thing in every file, with the Stata copy simply wearing its .a/.b form. In SPSS terms, your MISSING VALUES definitions survived the Save As. (Age itself carries no missing codes вҖ“ thatвҖҷs exactly why it makes a clean test variable here.)









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





For a clean variable like Age, base R agrees exactly: mean(MyData_r$Age) returns the same 40.65049. The difference appears with variables that carry declared missing-value codes, like Income вҖ“ those codes are ordinary numbers to base R, and the blockвҖҷs last lines show the damage live: mean(MyData_spss$Income) folds the -99s and -98s straight into the average, while jdesc() reads the declarations and leaves the codes out automatically вҖ“ provided the declarations exist: here they traveled with the files, and when a dataset arrives with codes that were never declared, you add the declarations yourself with jstatsвҖҷs jdeclare_udm() function. ItвҖҷs the same protection youвҖҷd expect from commercial statistical software.









NoteWhatвҖҷs the point?





The download at the bottom of this page is the finished version of what you built вҖ“ and finished means something specific. A goal every experienced programmer and statistician takes for granted, but new researchers are rarely told: a completed script should run from beginning to end without errors. On this page you experimented вҖ“ deliberate errors and prompts included, because thatвҖҷs how working scripts develop. The download shows what the end of that process looks like, and it differs from the page blocks in three principled ways: the deliberate error lines are commented out, the y/n prompt line is commented out (a prompt would pause an unattended run), and the saves вҖ“ plus the one reload that could collide with an earlier runвҖҷs leftovers вҖ“ carry overwrite = TRUE so the whole file can be run again and again, exactly the automatic-execution override from block 6.
To prove a script is finished, use the Source icon (to the right of Run in the script toolbar): it runs the entire file, top to bottom, in one go. A few things to know. Source shows the output without echoing each command back to the Console вҖ“ thatвҖҷs normal вҖ“ and the small arrow beside Source offers вҖңSource with EchoвҖқ if youвҖҷd like the commands shown too. jstats functions print their own tables, so their output always appears; but a bare base R line that merely displays a value вҖ“ ls(), getwd(), mean() вҖ“ stays quiet under Source, because R only auto-prints results when you run lines interactively вҖ“ and even interactively, what it prints is a bare, unlabeled number. ThatвҖҷs why the script prints its base R mean with cat(), a label, and a few blank lines to set it apart вҖ“ hand-formatting that jstats tables do for you. And if you re-run the script you pasted together yourself, the saves will refuse to overwrite their earlier files вҖ“ thatвҖҷs block 6вҖҷs lesson again; add overwrite = TRUE just as the finished file does.
The What you should see boxes under some blocks above reproduce that Console вҖ“ the blue command lines, the gray notices, the colored tables, a red error where one is meant to happen. They show the code being run, not the ## comment lines (running a comment just echoes it back and does nothing), so the box stays focused on what actually happens. Reading a block? The commented code is in the gray block; the result is in the box beneath it. Several boxes also picture your Environment pane вҖ“ the upper-right one вҖ“ so you can watch objects appear and disappear as you work. And where a file path appears, the boxes print ~/your-working-directory/ in place of a real folder name: on your own screen that reads as the actual path to your project.
Nothing needs deleting between runs, and nothing is deleted: the overwrite arguments handle the repeats, and the Data folder with its three saved files stays put after every run вҖ“ thatвҖҷs what saving is for. Remove the files by hand if you ever want a clean slate.
One more setup idea if youвҖҷre keeping the file вҖ“ and itвҖҷs worth keeping: finished scripts travel. Sooner or later youвҖҷll share one with a supervisor or collaborator, or receive one from someone else, and a script that runs top to bottom on another personвҖҷs computer is the standard to aim for. So a downloaded script deserves a proper home: jstats created the Data folder for you during the run-through, but you can also make folders yourself in RStudio вҖ“ in the Files tab of the lower-right pane, click New Folder and type a name into the popup. вҖңScriptsвҖқ is a good one for storing this download, and it will appear right beside the Data folder you created by following the steps above.

title: jstats
text:
This page is a hands-on run-through of moving data in and out of jstats: loading files, saving your work back out, and converting between formats.






NoteBefore you start





To follow along youвҖҷll need:

R, RStudio, and jstats installed. If theyвҖҷre not, work through Get Started, Steps 1-5 first and come back.
An RStudio Project open, so your working directory and files are already set up.
A new, empty R script open (File > New File > R Script). YouвҖҷll copy each numbered block into it, one at a time, running each before moving on.










NoteHow to Use this Page





The blocks on this page build one script. Paste each block into the same R script file as you reach it, in order вҖ” by the end, the whole file runs cleanly from top to bottom, except in the few places where a comment says an error is deliberate (so you can see what one looks like).
A few mechanics worth knowing:

Every block starts with a dashed comment header (## ---- 1. ... ----). It marks where one block ends and the next begins in your growing script вҖ” and RStudio reads those headers as sections, so they appear in the scriptвҖҷs outline for easy jumping around.
Experiment freely as you go вҖ” change a number, rerun a line, poke at things. Building the script yourself is the point.
We recommend working through the blocks one at a time first вҖ“ you learn far more by building the script yourself and watching each piece run than by reading a finished file. Once youвҖҷve done that (or if youвҖҷd rather grab everything at once), the complete, clean script вҖ“ without your experiments, and finished so it runs top to bottom вҖ“ is available to download at the bottom of the page.

This box is the same on every hands-on page in this group, so once youвҖҷve read it here, you can skip it elsewhere.









TipNew to R?





You donвҖҷt need to memorize any of this yet. Right now the goal is simply to run each line and watch what happens вҖ“ the ideas settle in as you go. A few things R doesnвҖҷt care about, so you donвҖҷt have to either: which line numbers your code lands on, and how many blank lines sit between commands вҖ“ paste each block anywhere below your previous code and it all works the same. Lines starting with # are comments: notes for humans that R skips entirely (extra hash marks, like ##, are still just a comment). And running a line a second time is almost always harmless вҖ“ youвҖҷll simply get the same result again. If a line doesnвҖҷt work, check that jstats loaded without errors, then try it again.









NoteReset your session





Experimenting means the occasional wrong turn вҖ“ a setting changed, the practice dataset overwritten, output that suddenly looks different. When something feels off and youвҖҷd like a clean footing without restarting RStudio, run these two lines in the Console:
## Put jstats' output settings back to their defaults
joutput("standard")   # output level, and decimal places back to 3

## Point saving and loading back at your working directory
joptions(data.dir = "")
That resets jstats itself. To clear away objects you created while following along вҖ“ data frames like MyData_r, or a value like MyObject вҖ“ the simplest way is the little broom icon at the top of the Environment pane (upper-right): one click sweeps the pane clean. It clears everything in the Environment, so use it when youвҖҷre between run-throughs, not in the middle of one. (You can also remove a single object by name, e.g.В rm(MyData_r).) The built-in practice data comes right back with jload("community") whenever you want it.
This box is the same on every hands-on page in this group.





WeвҖҷll start by loading the package and one of its built-in practice datasets.
## ---- 1. Load the package and a dataset ---------------------------------

## Load the jstats library (needed once per R session)
library(jstats)

## Load the package dataset called community
## It's loaded as a data frame into the Environment
jload("community")






NoteWhat you should see






The command lines are shown; the ## comment lines arenвҖҷt вҖ“ running a comment just echoes it back and does nothing.


Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminalГ—Background JobsГ—> library(jstats)
> jload("community")
Loaded the jstats example dataset 'community'.
5 variables have user-defined missing values:
  Income: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Education: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Smoker: -99 ["Refused"]
  Environment1: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Environment3: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
These codes are excluded as missing in jstats analyses. For better base R compatibility, convert them:
  jconvert(community, to = "stata")  - retains missing-value codes, base R compatible (recommended)
  jconvert(community, to = "baseR")  - converts to plain NA and removes missing-value codes
> |

Environment (upper-right pane)


EnvironmentHistoryConnectionsTutorial


Data


community103 obs. of 15 variables












NoteWhatвҖҷs the point?





library(jstats) switches the package on for the current session вҖ“ and once per session is all it takes. If youвҖҷve just come from the Quick Start page, jstats may well be loaded already; running the line again is harmless (it simply does nothing the second time). You can check at any point in RStudioвҖҷs Packages tab (lower-right pane): the checkbox beside jstats is ticked while the package is loaded. On a fresh load, jstats also prints a short version message in the Console; if it was already loaded, the line stays quiet. And if youвҖҷd rather never type the line again, R can run it for you at the start of every session вҖ“ the Loading jstats automatically page shows how.









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





For a dataset that ships inside a package, base R has one too: data(community) loads it. The difference shows up beyond package data: data() only reaches datasets bundled in packages, while jload() is one loader for everything on this page вҖ“ package datasets, your saved .rds files, and SPSS, Stata, SAS, Excel, and CSV files вҖ“ with the same one-line call.






Anything you create in R is stored as an object, and objects live in the Global Environment вҖ“ the workspace RStudio shows you in the Environment tab of the upper-right pane. Here we make two objects of our own, and open a second dataset alongside the first.
## ---- 2. Objects, the assignment operator, and the Global Environment ----

## "Objects" other than data frames can go into the Environment
## The "Assignment Operator" (less-than followed by a dash) is a key Base R fundamental
## Take the thing on the right and assign it to what's on the left
## Take the Region variable from the community data frame and assign it to a new object
## The new object we'll call "MyObject"
MyObject <- community$Region ## (Base R code)

## The name on the left is entirely yours to choose, and the thing on the
## right can be anything at all -- here, just the number 5
x <- 5

## You are not limited to one dataset at a time
## Load a second package dataset, clinic, alongside community
jload("clinic")






NoteWhat you should see






Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminalГ—Background JobsГ—> MyObject <- community$Region
> x <- 5
> jload("clinic")

Suspected missing-value codes detected:
  MoodRating:  -99 (4), -98 (3)
  Anxiety2:    -99 (3), -98 (3)

These codes are not formally defined, so they are not treated as missing.
Declare them as missing if they are; leave as-is if real.

# To declare one variable's codes as missing:

TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





The Global Environment has no real SPSS counterpart, and that is the most useful thing to know about it. In SPSS everything lives inside a dataset: COMPUTE x = 5. does not create an object called x вҖ“ it adds a column of 5s, one value per case, to whatever dataset is active. There is nowhere for a single value, or a single variable, to sit on its own. (SPSS macros come closest, and hardly anyone uses them.) In R the Environment is exactly that place: a workspace holding objects of any shape, and a data frame is simply one of the shapes.
SPSS can have several datasets open too вҖ“ but each opens in its own Data Editor window, and switching the active one takes a syntax chain most users never bother with:
GET FILE='community.sav'.
DATASET NAME community.
GET FILE='clinic.sav'.
DATASET NAME clinic.
DATASET ACTIVATE community.
In R there are no windows to juggle: the Environment holds several data frames side by side, and every command simply names the one it uses вҖ“ the $ in community$Region means вҖңthe Region column, in the community dataset.вҖқ






Objects you no longer need can be removed, and there are two ways to do it. rm() takes out exactly what you name and leaves everything else alone вҖ“ which is what you want when one object is wrong and the others are fine. The broom in the Environment paneвҖҷs toolbar takes out everything, in one click; RStudio asks you to confirm before it does. The code below uses rm(); the red arrow in the box points out the broom.
One thing to expect: rm() says nothing at all when it works. Silence is success вҖ“ the Environment pane is where you check.

## Remove the community data frame
rm(community)

## Remove the clinic data frame
rm(clinic)

NoteAside: how R writes file paths





A path is a folderвҖҷs address on your computer вҖ“ the folders you would click through, written out on one line. Three spelling quirks are worth thirty seconds each, because you will meet all three on this page.
The ~ shorthand. Look at the top of your Console: RStudio shows your working directory with a squiggle, something like ~/your-working-directory/. The ~ is shorthand for your home folder (on Windows, usually your Documents folder). getwd() prints that same address written out in full вҖ“ beginning, on Windows, with a drive letter such as C: or E: that names the disk. Short spelling in the Console header, long spelling from getwd(). Same place.
You have met that squiggle before, on the Quick Start page, doing a completely different job вҖ“ writing a model, as in jt(Optimism ~ Gender). In a file path it has nothing to do with models. Same character, two unrelated jobs.
The slashes. R writes paths with forward slashes (/) on every kind of computer. Windows itself writes the same address with backslashes: File Explorer shows E:\00 R Projects, R shows E:/00 R Projects. Same folder, two spellings. When you type a path in R, use /.
So: ~ means home, / goes between folders, and getwd() tells you the full address of where you are working.
And if all of that felt like a lot, donвҖҷt worry. You will not have to type a path anywhere on this page. As long as you work inside an RStudio Project вҖ“ which is what Before you start asks of you вҖ“ jstats handles the addresses for you. In a moment you will name a folder once, with joptions(data.dir = "Data"), and jstats creates it inside your project; from then on you load and save with plain file names like "MyData_r.rds", and jstats works out where they belong. Paths are worth recognizing when a message prints one. They are not something you need to write.









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





? is base R вҖ“ ?jload, ?ls, and ?mean all work the same way, for base functions and for functions from any installed package. (In RStudio, putting the cursor on a function name and pressing F1 does the same thing. On Macs the key varies from machine to machine вҖ“ F1 on some, fn + F1 on others, and on some Macs the function keys must first be enabled in System Settings.) What a base R setup doesnвҖҷt have is a grouped index: a typical workflow spans functions scattered across many packages, each with its own alphabetical help listing. ?jstats shows the whole toolkit on one page, organized by purpose вҖ“ one advantage of the functions traveling together as a package.






When you load a dataset thatвҖҷs already open, jstats checks with you before overwriting it вҖ“ watch for the y/n prompt in the Console window.
## ---- 6. The overwrite prompt -------------------------------------------

## The output you get in the Console Pane can include a confirmation prompt
## Here, jstats will not overwrite community unless you give it permission
jload("community") # Look at the y/n prompt in the console

## Set an optional overwrite argument/input to overwrite without the prompt
jload("community", overwrite = TRUE)






NoteWhatвҖҷs the point?





The overwrite argument is here to show you two things:

What an argument looks like. Options go inside the parentheses as name = value вҖ“ here, overwrite = TRUE.
Prompts can be overridden. The few times jstats asks for confirmation, the prompt appears in the Console вҖ“ and each one can be switched off with the right argument. That matters when code runs automatically, with nobody at the keyboard to answer: it happens in several places behind this website, and in the finished script you can download at the bottom of this page.










NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





Base R doesnвҖҷt ask. Loading or assigning over an existing object replaces it silently вҖ“ no prompt, no warning, and the old copy is gone. The confirmation prompt is a jstats safeguard.






LetвҖҷs save your work вҖ“ first with no folder specified, to see where files go by default, and then to a Data folder of your own.
## ---- 7. Saving to a Data folder ----------------------------------------

## Use the assignment operator to copy community to a new data frame called MyData
MyData <- community

## Save it with no folder specified -- where does the file go?
## The console message shows the full path: it landed in your project's
## working directory, the default location
## It's the same path the Base R getwd() command showed you earlier
jsave(MyData, "WhereDidIGo.rds")

## Delete that test file
## file.remove() is base R -- the file-on-disk equivalent of the rm()
## you used for data frames. It replies with a cryptic [1] TRUE
file.remove("WhereDidIGo.rds")

## Now name a Data folder of your own
## jstats will create this folder upon first save
## Or you can create it manually
joptions(data.dir = "Data")

## Save the dataset to the Data folder and call it MyData_r.rds
## Specify R native format with the .rds extension
## The Data folder is created now, on this first save
jsave(MyData, "MyData_r.rds")

NoteWhatвҖҷs the point?





Why set a data folder at all? Tidiness now, and a payoff later. With data.dir set, every save and load on this page knows where to look. And thatвҖҷs the habit to keep: put joptions(data.dir = "Data") near the top of your own scripts, then drop a dataset of yours into that folder and jload("YourFile.sav") opens it by name alone. If youвҖҷre not sure where the folder is on your computer, the jsave message has already told you вҖ“ it prints the full path each time, ready to paste into File Explorer (Windows) or find in Finder (Mac).









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





Saving to the working directory by default is base RвҖҷs behavior too вҖ“ saveRDS(MyData, "MyData_r.rds") puts the file in the same place; jstats just tells you, in full, where it went. To use a folder, base R wonвҖҷt create it for you (thatвҖҷs dir.create("Data") first), and the folder name gets typed into every path from then on вҖ“ with any subfolders along for the ride, e.g. saveRDS(MyData, "Data/CommunityStudy/Wave1/MyData_r.rds"). joptions(data.dir = ...) sets the location once, and every jsave() and jload() after that already knows where to look.
And note what file.remove() didnвҖҷt do: ask. Like the silent overwrite in block 6, base R deletes with no confirmation вҖ“ mix up a filename and a real data file is gone. jstatsвҖҷs prompts exist precisely because of edges like that.

TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





In SPSS, user-missing values are declared in Variable View and travel with the file. When you Save As a different format, those definitions donвҖҷt always carry across cleanly. jstats keeps the equivalent information with your data, and when a format needs it in a different form, jconvert() does the translation вҖ“ which is why saving straight to Stata format above stops and asks you to convert first.









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





Base R alone canвҖҷt write SPSS or Stata files. YouвҖҷd install and load the haven package and call haven::write_sav() or haven::write_dta() yourself вҖ“ and haven is exactly the proven library jstats uses under the hood, so nothing here reinvents the wheel. What jstats spares you is the setup and the sharp edge: loading an extra package in every script, learning its separate function set, and handling the missing-value translation by hand вҖ“ haven refuses SPSS-style missing codes headed into Stata format, just as you saw above, and converting them yourself is genuinely fiddly. jconvert() is that translation, done for you.
Two conversion habits are worth knowing even if youвҖҷre an experienced R user.

Missing                                                
.b ["Don't know"]           3     2.91       --      --
.a ["Refused"]              3     2.91       --      --
                                                       
Total                     103   100.00                 


> |






One more control before the final check: how many decimal places jstats shows. The default setting is 3, the maximum is 7 вҖ“ and for what comes next, we want all 7. (One exception: p-values keep their own fixed three-decimal convention and ignore this setting.)
## ---- 11. Turning up the digits -----------------------------------------

## By default, jstats shows up to 3 decimal places
jdesc(MyData_r, Age)   # see the Age mean below -- the callouts explain the decimals

## Turn the display up to the maximum of 7 decimal places
joutput(digits = 7)
jdesc(MyData_r, Age)   # the same mean, now with more decimals shown






NoteWhat you should see






Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminalГ—Background JobsГ—> jdesc(MyData_r, Age)

Age         103          103   18   71  40.65  11.62

Age         103          103   18   71  40.6504854  11.619548

> |









TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





SPSS output shows a fixed number of decimals, padding with zeros to fill the width вҖ“ a mean of 40.65 appears as 40.650 in a three-decimal table. jstats goes the other way: it never prints trailing zeros, so digits sets a maximum, and each column shows only the decimals it actually needs. ThatвҖҷs why the mean above reads 40.65 at the default setting of 3 вҖ“ its third decimal happens to be a zero. And if a statistic lands exactly on a round value, it displays short even at digits = 7: thatвҖҷs trimming, not truncation or a stuck setting.









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





The closest is options(digits = ...), and itвҖҷs a different animal: it sets significant digits, globally, for nearly everything R prints. joutput(digits = ...) means decimal places, and it changes only jstats output.






Those three data frames each made a round trip through a different file format. If the trips were truly lossless, the same statistics should come back from every one of them вҖ“ right down to the seventh decimal.
## ---- 12. The proof that nothing was lost -------------------------------

## Ask for the mean of Age from each of the three reloaded data frames
jdesc(MyData_r, Age)
jdesc(MyData_spss, Age)
jdesc(MyData_stata, Age)
## All three report the identical mean, to the last decimal:
## same data, three file formats, nothing lost along the way

## And one base R caution, shown live (the box below explains):
## Income carries declared missing-value codes (-99, -98)
jdesc(MyData_spss, Income)   # the correct mean -- jdesc leaves the codes out
## Base R gives a bare, unlabeled number, so extra work is needed just to
## make it visible: cat() adds a label, and the "\n"s add blank lines to
## set it apart. Run line by line (interactively), a plain mean() prints
## only a cryptic [1] number; run with Source, it wouldn't appear at all
cat("\n\nBase R mean of Income:", mean(MyData_spss$Income), "\n\n\n")  # a poisoned mean

## Set the display back to the default before you go
joutput(digits = 3)

## Put the data folder setting back to its default (the working directory) too
## Passing "" clears it (data.dir = NULL would leave it unchanged)
## A finished script leaves your session exactly as it found it
joptions(data.dir = "")

TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





jdesc() is the counterpart of SPSSвҖҷs Analyze > Descriptive Statistics > Descriptives вҖ“ the same N, minimum, maximum, mean, and standard deviation youвҖҷd read from that output table. And the check above may feel familiar from practice: reopening a dataset thatвҖҷs been through another format, and confirming the descriptives still match.
The matching numbers are only half the story вҖ“ the missing-value declarations made the round trip too, which is arguably the bigger deal. You saw it back in block 10: EducationвҖҷs codes come through meaning the same thing in every file, with the Stata copy simply wearing its .a/.b form. In SPSS terms, your MISSING VALUES definitions survived the Save As. (Age itself carries no missing codes вҖ“ thatвҖҷs exactly why it makes a clean test variable here.)









NoteWhatвҖҷs the Base R equivalent?





For a clean variable like Age, base R agrees exactly: mean(MyData_r$Age) returns the same 40.65049. The difference appears with variables that carry declared missing-value codes, like Income вҖ“ those codes are ordinary numbers to base R, and the blockвҖҷs last lines show the damage live: mean(MyData_spss$Income) folds the -99s and -98s straight into the average, while jdesc() reads the declarations and leaves the codes out automatically вҖ“ provided the declarations exist: here they traveled with the files, and when a dataset arrives with codes that were never declared, you add the declarations yourself with jstatsвҖҷs jdeclare_udm() function. ItвҖҷs the same protection youвҖҷd expect from commercial statistical software.









NoteWhatвҖҷs the point?





The download at the bottom of this page is the finished version of what you built вҖ“ and finished means something specific. A goal every experienced programmer and statistician takes for granted, but new researchers are rarely told: a completed script should run from beginning to end without errors. On this page you experimented вҖ“ deliberate errors and prompts included, because thatвҖҷs how working scripts develop. The download shows what the end of that process looks like, and it differs from the page blocks in three principled ways: the deliberate error lines are commented out, the y/n prompt line is commented out (a prompt would pause an unattended run), and the saves вҖ“ plus the one reload that could collide with an earlier runвҖҷs leftovers вҖ“ carry overwrite = TRUE so the whole file can be run again and again, exactly the automatic-execution override from block 6.
To prove a script is finished, use the Source icon (to the right of Run in the script toolbar): it runs the entire file, top to bottom, in one go. A few things to know. Source shows the output without echoing each command back to the Console вҖ“ thatвҖҷs normal вҖ“ and the small arrow beside Source offers вҖңSource with EchoвҖқ if youвҖҷd like the commands shown too. jstats functions print their own tables, so their output always appears; but a bare base R line that merely displays a value вҖ“ ls(), getwd(), mean() вҖ“ stays quiet under Source, because R only auto-prints results when you run lines interactively вҖ“ and even interactively, what it prints is a bare, unlabeled number. ThatвҖҷs why the script prints its base R mean with cat(), a label, and a few blank lines to set it apart вҖ“ hand-formatting that jstats tables do for you. And if you re-run the script you pasted together yourself, the saves will refuse to overwrite their earlier files вҖ“ thatвҖҷs block 6вҖҷs lesson again; add overwrite = TRUE just as the finished file does.
The What you should see boxes under some blocks above reproduce that Console вҖ“ the blue command lines, the gray notices, the colored tables, a red error where one is meant to happen. They show the code being run, not the ## comment lines (running a comment just echoes it back and does nothing), so the box stays focused on what actually happens. Reading a block? The commented code is in the gray block; the result is in the box beneath it. Several boxes also picture your Environment pane вҖ“ the upper-right one вҖ“ so you can watch objects appear and disappear as you work. And where a file path appears, the boxes print ~/your-working-directory/ in place of a real folder name: on your own screen that reads as the actual path to your project.
Nothing needs deleting between runs, and nothing is deleted: the overwrite arguments handle the repeats, and the Data folder with its three saved files stays put after every run вҖ“ thatвҖҷs what saving is for. Remove the files by hand if you ever want a clean slate.
One more setup idea if youвҖҷre keeping the file вҖ“ and itвҖҷs worth keeping: finished scripts travel. Sooner or later youвҖҷll share one with a supervisor or collaborator, or receive one from someone else, and a script that runs top to bottom on another personвҖҷs computer is the standard to aim for. So a downloaded script deserves a proper home: jstats created the Data folder for you during the run-through, but you can also make folders yourself in RStudio вҖ“ in the Files tab of the lower-right pane, click New Folder and type a name into the popup. вҖңScriptsвҖқ is a good one for storing this download, and it will appear right beside the Data folder you created by following the steps above.

This page gets the jstats package installed and loaded, ready to use. It assumes youвҖҷve already installed R and RStudio and have an RStudio Project open. If you havenвҖҷt, work through Install R and RStudio and RStudio Orientation first, then come back here.






TipFor experienced R users





If youвҖҷve installed R packages before, this is ordinary install-and-load with one small wrinkle. jstats isnвҖҷt on CRAN yet, so it installs from an r-universe repository (a free, CRAN-like service that publishes ready-made package binaries). In practice that means a single install.packages() call with a repos argument вҖ” the r-universe repository for jstats itself, plus a regular CRAN mirror for any supporting packages вҖ” shown just below. Because youвҖҷre installing a binary, nothing is compiled, so thereвҖҷs no need for Rtools (Windows) or AppleвҖҷs command line tools (Mac), and no remotes or GitHub step. jstats also checks for a newer version each time it loads, and jupdate() updates it in a single command. Jump to the install line below вҖ” the rest is the install-and-load you already know.

title: jstats
text:
What you need first
Before the steps below, make sure you have:

R and RStudio installed вҖ” see Install R and RStudio.
An RStudio Project open вҖ” RStudio Orientation covers creating one.

title: jstats
text:
Install jstats and load it
jstats installs with a single command. Click in the Console (bottom-left pane), paste the two lines below, and press Enter вҖ” the first installs jstats, the second loads it:
install.packages("jstats",
  repos = c("https://jma61.r-universe.dev", "https://cloud.r-project.org"))
library(jstats)
You may see a red WARNING that Rtools is required. ThatвҖҷs normal and not an error вҖ” jstats installs without it.
The repos part just tells R where to look: the first address is the r-universe repository where jstats is published, and the second is an ordinary CRAN mirror that supplies any of jstatsвҖҷs supporting packages you donвҖҷt already have. Both hand over ready-made versions, so nothing is built from source and the install is quick.
Installing is normally a once-per-computer job вҖ” plus once more if you ever update R itself, which comes out in a new version about once a year and starts a fresh package library when it does. Worth bookmarking: this page always carries the current install line, so itвҖҷs the one to come back to on a new computer or after an R update. (If library(jstats) ever fails after updating R, the Troubleshooting page has the details.)
On the first install вҖ” or the first time you load jstats after updating R вҖ” you may see a line like package 'jstats' was built under R version 4.x.x. ThatвҖҷs normal and needs no action: it only means the ready-made copy was built on a slightly different R version than the one youвҖҷre running. It clears the next time you update R.
If it loaded cleanly, youвҖҷll see a short jstats startup message вҖ” and, if a newer version is already out, a note with the exact lines to update (more on that below). That message is how you know jstats is loaded and ready.
YouвҖҷll run library(jstats) at the start of each session. Prefer not to? You can set your Project to load it for you automatically вҖ” see Loading jstats automatically.

If the install fails
The most common snags are below вҖ” scan the titles and open the one that matches your error. If yours isnвҖҷt here, the full Troubleshooting page has more.






WarningThe install seems half-done or broken





The simplest fix is to remove jstats and install it fresh:
remove.packages("jstats")
install.packages("jstats",
  repos = c("https://jma61.r-universe.dev", "https://cloud.r-project.org"))
library(jstats)









WarningRStudio hangs or loops every time it starts





This almost always means an install command was put in your .Rprofile. That file runs every time R starts, so an install line tries to reinstall on every startup and can loop. Your .Rprofile should only ever hold lightweight startup lines like library(jstats) вҖ” never an install command. To recover: close RStudio, open your Project folder in your computerвҖҷs file manager, open the .Rprofile file in a plain text editor (Notepad on Windows, TextEdit on Mac), delete the install line, keeping the library(jstats) line (and its reminder comment, if your .Rprofile has one), save, and reopen RStudio.

title: jstats
text:
Keep jstats up to date
Because jstats is updated often during this phase, it checks for a newer version each time you load it with library(jstats). When one is available, it prints a short message at startup вҖ” the new version number and the one command to run. That command is:
jupdate()
Run it in the Console and jstats updates itself: one command, with nothing to copy or keep track of. jupdate() first checks that youвҖҷre online and that you arenвҖҷt already on the latest version (if you are, it just says so and stops), then downloads and installs the new version. It doesnвҖҷt pause to ask вҖңare you sure?вҖқ вҖ” it goes ahead and updates.
One thing to know: installing the new version and loading it are two separate steps. jupdate() puts the new version on your computer, but the session youвҖҷre in keeps running the old one until you restart R. You donвҖҷt have to remember how вҖ” when the update finishes, jupdate() prints the exact steps:
jstats has been updated.

Restart R to load it in RStudio:
  - open the Session menu > choose Restart R
  - or press Ctrl+Shift+F10

The Console will return to a blank prompt.
Reload jstats with library(jstats) (unless loaded automatically on startup).
Follow those and youвҖҷre running the new version.
(This all gets simpler once jstats reaches CRAN: jupdate() keeps working, just updating the ordinary way, and weвҖҷll revise these instructions then.)






NoteGoing deeper (optional)





A couple of extra options. If youвҖҷd rather confirm before anything installs, run jupdate(ask = TRUE) вҖ” it shows the available and installed versions and waits for your okay. Updates are reliable here because jupdate() installs in a separate R process, so the copy loaded in your session never has to overwrite its own files mid-update (the usual cause of a failed update on Windows). And to silence the startup check entirely вҖ” for instance on a machine thatвҖҷs on a network but has no internet вҖ” set options(jstats.check_updates = FALSE).









ImportantThis is a beta, and your input shapes it



While jstats is in this pre-release phase, itвҖҷs still actively growing вҖ” which makes now the best time to ask for things. If a function is missing an option your work needs, or youвҖҷd like an analysis it doesnвҖҷt yet cover, say so by email: SurveyCentre@griffith.edu.au. If you hit an error, include the full error message, what you ran, and which versions of R and jstats youвҖҷre on (you can get those by running R.version.string and packageVersion("jstats")) вҖ” that detail makes a fix far quicker. Changes are far easier to fold in now than after the package reaches its stable release, where keeping existing code working limits what can change.








TipFor experienced R users





A concrete example of feature-by-request: a researcher wanted to see which cells drive a significant chi-square, so adjusted standardized residuals were added to the cross-tabs function вҖ” jcrosstab(Education ~ Volunteer, data = community, residuals = "adjusted"). That kind of targeted addition is exactly what this phase is for.

title: jstats
section: What to expect at this early stage
text:
What to expect at this early stage
Using jstats during its pre-release phase comes with a simple, honest trade. The costs are small and real: the occasional error message that reads more cryptically than it should, and now and then a feature you wish were there but isnвҖҷt yet. The benefit is results fast вҖ” and, as the note above says, a direct line to shape what gets built next.
ThereвҖҷs also a safety net that makes the trade an easy one. jstats is a convenience layer, not a cage. Anything it doesnвҖҷt do, plain R still does вҖ” you can always drop back to base R, or reach for another package, right alongside jstats in the very same script. ThatвҖҷs the intended path: use jstats for the common social-science tasks itвҖҷs built for, pick up a little real R painlessly as you go, and when you eventually need something specialized it doesnвҖҷt cover, youвҖҷll have learned enough to add the package that does. YouвҖҷre never stuck, and youвҖҷre never learning a dead end.

With jstats installed and loaded, the jstats Quick Start puts it to work.

title: jstats
text:
Once jstats is installed, you can have your Project load it for you every time it opens вҖ” so you never type library(jstats) by hand. ItвҖҷs optional (typing the one line at the start of a session works perfectly well), but worth doing: besides saving the keystrokes, it means the packageвҖҷs automatic update check runs every time you start work вҖ” so youвҖҷre always told when a new version is available вҖ” and you open each session ready to go. This uses a small setup file called an .Rprofile (a file R reads automatically when it starts).
Create a plain text file named .Rprofile in your Project folder containing these two lines:
# To reinstall jstats (new computer, or after updating R) see the install code at: jma61.github.io/jstats-guides/install-jstats.html
library(jstats)
The second line does the loading. The first is a comment вҖ” R ignores any line that starts with # вҖ” holding the address of the install page, so the reinstall instructions are one glance away on a new computer or after an R update. Keep the # in place: without it, R would try to run the address as a command at every startup and complain; with it, the line is just a note that costs nothing.
Save it, then restart R (Session menu, Restart R). From now on, every session in this Project starts with jstats already loaded.
Keep this to lightweight startup lines вҖ” loading a package, and later a setting or two. Do not put an install command (an install.packages("jstats", ...) line) in .Rprofile: because the file runs on every startup, an install line there tries to reinstall each time and can send RStudio into a loop. Installing is a one-off you run by hand; loading is the repeatable step that belongs in startup.
WeвҖҷre keeping this minimal for now. Later youвҖҷll add a line or two more вҖ” where your data lives, and how many digits to show in output вҖ” but this is plenty to start.






NoteGoing deeper (optional)





This .Rprofile lives inside your Project and applies only to this Project. ThatвҖҷs deliberate вҖ” itвҖҷs self-contained and safe to edit. ThereвҖҷs also a global, all-Projects version, but itвҖҷs better left until youвҖҷre juggling several Projects at once; the books cover it.

title: jstats
text:
Below are the jstats functions and datasets, grouped by what they do. First, two quick notes on getting help.


Every function has a built-in help page. The quick way to open one in RStudio is to click the functionвҖҷs name вҖ” in your script or the console вҖ” and press F1 (or Fn+F1 on most Macs). In any R setup you can also type ? followed by the name вҖ” for example, ?jfreq. Either way you get the description, the options, and worked examples.



The function and dataset names below link to those same help pages online вҖ” and those pages rebuild themselves automatically from the package. Whenever the package changes or a new function is added, they regenerate from its built-in documentation and the examples are re-run, so the documentation and the code never drift apart: what you see is always current. The payoff is that every example shows the real output the call produces, not output typed in by hand вҖ” so you can copy one and trust it matches what youвҖҷll get.






NoteGrowing into a fuller reference



Over time this section will grow into a task-indexed reference вҖ” a place to look up how to do a specific thing while you work, organized by what you want to do rather than by function name. For now, itвҖҷs a grouped map of whatвҖҷs available, with each name linking to its full help page.






jdesc вҖ” descriptive statistics for one or more variables.
jfreq вҖ” frequency tables for categorical variables.
jscreen вҖ” a one-call overview of a whole dataset: ranges, missing values, possible outliers, and what kind each variable looks to be.





jt вҖ” independent-samples or paired-samples t-test.
jaov вҖ” one-way ANOVA.
jcrosstab вҖ” cross-tabulation, with an optional chi-square test.
jcorr вҖ” a correlation matrix, with p-values and the N behind each pair.
jlm вҖ” linear regression.
jlogistic вҖ” logistic regression.





jalpha вҖ” CronbachвҖҷs alpha reliability analysis for a set of scale items.
jsum вҖ” a row-wise sum across several variables (for example, a scale total).
javg вҖ” a row-wise mean across several variables (for example, a scale score).





jrecode вҖ” recode a variable using an explicit value mapping, with optional labels.
jrelabel вҖ” add or change a variableвҖҷs variable- and value-labels.
jdeclare_udm вҖ” declare user-defined missing values (values that should count as missing, such as a вҖңRefusedвҖқ code) without overwriting your data.
jsubset вҖ” set a case-selection filter so functions run on a chosen subset of cases.
jcomplete вҖ” set a listwise complete-case filter so a run of analyses share a matching N.
jcopy вҖ” copy a data frame, carrying any of its variable settings along with it.





jload вҖ” open a data file (from commercial statistical software, Excel, or CSV) in one command, bringing its value labels, variable labels, and missing-value codes along automatically.
jsave вҖ” save a data frame back out to a file.
jconvert вҖ” convert missing-value declarations between the conventions different programs use.
jdata_dir вҖ” show the folder jstats reads your data files from.





juse вҖ” set (or display) the default dataset, so you donвҖҷt have to name it in every call.
joptions вҖ” set or display session-level options, such as the data folder.
joutput вҖ” set how much detail jstats prints (the output verbosity level).
jupdate вҖ” update jstats to the latest version (one command; restart R afterward to load it).





jplot вҖ” plot a jstats result, or draw a quick plot of variables straight from a dataset.




Most users will not need these. jstats works out what kind each variable is on its own; these let you override that when you want a particular variable handled a particular way.

jnumeric вҖ” treat a variable as numeric.
jdummy вҖ” treat a categorical variable as a set of dummy variables in regression.
jcount вҖ” treat a variable as a count.
jlikert вҖ” treat a variable as an ordered (Likert) response item.




jstats ships with two practice datasets, both available the moment the package loads:

community вҖ” a clean 100-respondent community survey (income, education, age, a wellbeing score, several yes/no items, a region, and a five-item attitude scale). The packageвҖҷs default example data; itвҖҷs what every functionвҖҷs help examples use.
clinic вҖ” a deliberately messy 70-client mental-health and wellbeing sample, the companion for practicing data cleaning: it carries undeclared missing-value codes, stripped labels, and imperfect scale items to work through.




A few capabilities are planned for future versions вҖ” among them regression for count outcomes (Poisson and negative-binomial models) and exporting formatted, APA-style results to Word. The function set above is what ships today.
